channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Geography – a bridge between the pure and soft sciences
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Akanksha

When I was teaching the composition of the atmosphere, one of my students asked me “Ma'am isn’t this chemistry? Why are you teaching this in geography?”

The student had studied the same topic in her chemistry class.

After a brief pause I asked her, “Child, do you know what geography is?”

She said, “Yes, it is the study of mountains, plateaus, maps, etc.”

I could only smile at her narrow understanding of the subject and decided that my next class would solely be designed to bust such misconceptions about the subject.

I drew a simple Venn diagram to show the main divisions in geography – physical and human. The diagram showed that geography is an all-encompassing subject, which meant that popular subjects could all be studied from a geographical perspective. My students and I spent that entire class discussing the Venn diagram.

Physical geography is the concoction of pure sciences: physics, chemistry, and biology, whereas human geography is much more diverse and mainly includes demographics, social, political, economic, and settlement geography.

Teaching these branches of geography requires different skillsets as physical geography is more visual and human geography provides a platform to build perspectives through debates and discussions.

It is a perfect example of an interdisciplinary subject, where an educator and students must connect the dots and apply their learnings from various disciplines. It can’t be studied in isolation.

In my years as a geography teacher, I have realized that the right approach to deal with the subject is as follows:

Firstly, an educator must be observant about everything. This skill works like magic when you explain a concept with relevant examples.

Let’s say we visit a restaurant and order a Rajasthani thali. We will relish so many dishes made of chickpea flour, curd based gravies, and dried vegetables. The thali tells us that this region lacks in vegetation cover due to its high temperatures and low humidity. Temperatures are high in Rajasthan as it is a desert and rapid evaporation leads to low humidity. The teacher can then talk about how people have adapted to these harsh conditions. The Khair tree is a living example of resilience as every part of the tree is used by the people of Rajasthan and without the ker sangri dish, the Rajasthani thali is incomplete. Then, the settlement pattern, clothing, occupations, etc., can be discussed.

Secondly, geography is a spaciotemporal discipline as it studies how things change over time and space. Therefore, teaching geography using an evolutionary approach gives a holistic coverage to the subject.

Thirdly, understanding the demand of the topic is crucial as each topic must be dealt using a certain approach. For example, while teaching the types of clouds, an observation worksheet can be designed, where students can be asked to observe clouds for a week and then enter their observations in the worksheet. When they learn like this, they will remember things for longer.

Current affairs can easily be amalgamated in geography. For example, while teaching world geography, one can show countries of the world in an atlas or wall map where major geopolitical tensions are ongoing, giving students a better understanding of the historical underpinnings, distribution of world resources and disputed areas owing to the expansion policy of a few nations.

Similarly, geography teachers can use the geological time scale as a tool to trace past events and evolution with time, especially for topics like volcanoes, rocks, physical features, biogeography, etc.

Any subject can be taught from a geographical perspective – all you need is passion and the right pedagogy to keep your inner geographer alive!

Akanksha is a freelance educator based in Bengaluru. She graduated in Geography (hons.) from the prestigious Miranda House, Delhi University. She has 8 years of teaching experience. Currently, she is working as a geography teacher and UPSC coach in Bengaluru. She is passionate about her subject and is working to develop a good pedagogy and resources for teaching-learning geography. She can be reached at akankshaakki1416@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Akanksha
Would you think of geography as an interdisciplinary subject? Akanksha writes about the physical and human divisions of the subject and how these can be taught in interesting yet relatable ways.

item.title: School geography: text and pedagogy
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sachidanand Sinha

Textbooks produced by central, state, and private institutions provide the basis for teaching and learning, particularly in schools in India. Various education boards such as the CBSE, ICSE, and state boards approve the curricular structure, and textbooks are written accordingly. One may observe significant variations from board to board in the curriculum and in the pedagogy enshrined and presented in the content. This needs to be appreciated in view of the environmental and social diversities across and within the states of India. In this article, I intend to examine some aspects of the school geography curriculum and textbooks produced by the National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT). It may be noted that several school boards have fully adopted NCERT textbooks in their schools. My reading of the curriculum and contents of the geography textbooks/sections are sporadic and selective. It may also be noted that the curriculum structure and content of school textbooks are currently under revision in light of the recommendations of NEP 2020. Only one textbook of social studies for class VI has been published by the NCERT so far, and there is nothing available in the public domain about the CBSE curricular structure for the upper primary, secondary, and senior secondary classes. I understand that social studies textbooks for classes VII and VIII will be published by the NCERT in a few months. Therefore, I limit my reflections to the class VI textbook, which is available on the NCERT website.

The key issues on which I have attempted content analysis is largely based on textual reading pertaining to the following:

  1. What are the key learning objectives of the chapters and themes dealing with geography?
  2. What are the activities and projects suggested in the book that may creatively engage children and allow them to reflect?
  3. Are the chapters in sync with contemporary knowledge in geography and to what extent do the themes permit lateral conversation with other themes in social studies?

Traditions of geography and contemporary concerns

Let me reflect upon what the discipline of geography is expected to contribute towards our understanding of the society and physical environment. In 1964, Pattison[1] presented a summary of geographic traditions in American education, which are the following:

• Spatial tradition – Focuses on location, mapping, and spatial relationships.

• Area studies tradition – Emphasizes place, region, and cultural characteristics.

• Human-land tradition or human-environment relations – Explores human-environment interactions.

• Earth science tradition – Concentrates on physical geography and the Earth’s processes.

One can say that the above traditions also signify developments within the discipline not in the order presented by Pattison but perhaps in the reverse, where the earth science tradition was first to become the prominent practice along with the human-land tradition. The area studies, popularly known as the regional geography tradition became more significant due to the works and teachings of Blache[i], Hettner[ii], and Hartshorne[iii] during the late 19th and the first half of the 20th century. It began to decline after the famous Hartshorne-Schaefer debate[iv] and the emergence of the spatial tradition in geography. It will not be correct to assume that the spatial tradition was absent prior to the 1950s. In fact, this tradition could be seen in the works of Strabo (64 BC-20 AD) and much later in the writings of the founding fathers of geography Alexander von Humboldt (1769-1859) and Carl Ritter (1779-1859).

The spatial tradition began to acquire strong theoretical and empirical bases following Walter Christaller’s Central Place theory (1893-1969).

The dynamism of the discipline could be seen in the emergence of a variety of sub-disciplines and approaches over the years, defining its changing nature. From cosmography – the description of the universe (cosmos) to chorology – describing places on various scales, to geography as the study of space and place, the discipline has evolved and transformed remarkably, though the central concepts have fundamentally remained the same as presented by Pattison. Figures 1 and 2 present synoptic representations of the thematic and topical classification of the sub-disciplines in geography (as practiced in the 1960s) and schools of modern human geography. The question is whether school curriculum, textbooks, and teaching practices in India address the contemporary concerns of geography.

There is no doubt that the modern and contemporary concerns in geography have evolved around the complexities of deciphering space and place. Concepts such as areal or spatial variation, areal associations, spatial relations, spatial interdependence, spatial diffusion, and spatial organizations have been profitably employed not only to refer to the where of elements in space and place but also to explain why not there and what they may imply in so far as human and social life was concerned. “The term ‘space’ indicates a location that can be identified and fixed…space is (thus) objective. In contrast, the term ‘place’ refers to how people feel about or give meaning to specific locations, in other words subjective…. The two terms are used in a variety of ways, but their key contribution is to show the interrelatedness of space and the social: that means social relations and interactions affect or influence space, and that space in turn affects or influences the social…space is not just an objective location, but it is actively created and/or changed by people, and at the same time makes social lives.”[2] This quote adequately defines what spatiality is. Doreen Massey3 rightly maintains that spaces and places are always in the process of being made, or produced and socially constructed, as Lefebvre attempts to understand the process of space production under capitalism. This would involve temporality as an important axis to understand change, hence Soja’s4 triad of spatiality, historicality, and sociality acquires central concerns in contemporary human geography.

Reflections of the NCERT geography textbook

In this section, I provide some reflections on select chapters of the social science textbook Exploring Society – India and Beyond produced last year by the NCERT based on the curricular framework for class VI. Geography at this stage of school education is part of the social sciences curriculum. The text is organized around five themes, of which Theme A titled India and the World – Land and the People is related to geography. This theme has three chapters preceded by a section on ‘Why Social Science’. The three chapters under Theme A “…include the basics of the geographical world around us – some of the main features of our planet, and the way to represent them on a map…. It also asks how geographical features – oceans, mountains, rivers, etc. – have shaped entire civilizations throughout their histories. It is also, in India’s case, about how its natural setting has contributed to giving this ancient civilization a unique identity.” (p.4). One chapter under Theme C: Our Cultural Heritage and Knowledge Traditions, deals with Unity in Diversity (Chapter 8), which also has geographical significance.

The introductory chapter Why Social Science, I feel is highly misplaced and unscientific for children aged 11-12 years. The chapter begins with two images in rural and urban settings, which only represent some natural and built spaces and are completely devoid of people who form the central subject of social science studies. Can this be construed as a marginal issue? Children are visual learners and selection of images can be vital to their understanding at the entry point. I would have liked a sketch showing how social life in various socio-economic and environmental settings looks like. The paragraphs that follow attempt to explain what the social sciences are all about. Here, one comes across phrases such as wars, conflicts, destruction of the natural environment, and several other problems with just one line on technological progress (without even defining). Later in the text, one comes across two phrases ‘great possibilities’ and ‘great challenges’. The sequence of possibilities and challenges is flawed. It’s obvious that human societies have always responded to challenges and created opportunities and possibilities for their security and survival.

One is rather aghast to come across a narrow perspective of what science is all about: “You may wonder whether this is a ‘science’ like, say, physics or chemistry. It is not. The discipline does use scientific methods wherever possible (you will see a few examples in this textbook), but its focus – human society – is, again, too diverse to allow the kind of set procedures and fixed results the sciences come up with.” This debate has been long going. It’s true that social sciences are not like physics and chemistry, but every branch of knowledge has developed scientific methods to address the problems they study. To say that social sciences do not use scientific methods is not entirely correct. Today, we stand at a juncture where physics, chemistry, language, literature, mathematics and behavioural sciences stand together in developing Artificial Intelligence based technologies, which would not have been possible without the use of scientific methods. Also, look at the italicized text in the above quote, does it not imply that set procedures as required by science cannot be followed with the social sciences because the human society is ‘too diverse’? Is diversity the cause of social science being ‘unscientific’? Every discipline has its own procedures, that keep developing, to analyze results from data collated using various methods. Is ’subjective’ not scientific? The diversity of human societies has been responsible for developing and adapting technologies suitable to their environmental and social conditions. These have provided us with the knowledge of how one size may not fit all. This presents an extremely restricted and negative meaning of human diversity.

Next, I take up the case of Chapter 8: Unity in Diversity from the same textbook. The authors have attempted to describe diversity in terms of foods, textiles and clothing, and festivals and literature (epics of Ramayana and Mahabharata). Diversity in India is multilayered and is a product of influences that are both endogenic and external, presenting a synthesis of cultures from time immemorial. The authors have begun well by mentioning that India has a fantastic linguistic diversity. But they fail to build on this beginning. Let me reflect upon two aspects of diversity talked about in the textbook – food and clothing. On food, the text is limited to enlisting the main food grains consumed in different parts of India. Is our food limited only to food grains and pulses? What about other items that comprise our staple diet? There is no mention of animal products such as meat, eggs, and fish. Even the vegetables are conspicuous by their absence. What comprises food on a Naga, Miri, Chenchu, Santhal, and Oraon platter? What about the densely populated long coastline of India, where people of all sections and identities relish fish? But diversity in sources of food such as rice, wheat, millets, pulses, vegetables, fruits and animal products is only one part of the story. The more substantive part is the development of culinary traditions, which has taken centuries to evolve through interaction with other cultures. Can we ignore the Malabari, Awadhi, Hyderabadi, Kolhapuri, Goan, Punajabi, Kashmiri or even the Naga, Manipuri, Meghalayan, Assamese, Benagali or Odia culinary traditions? The list is too huge to include here. There are maps already available on the internet which could serve to drive home not just diversity but also unity – a process of synthesis, borrowing from other cultures and integrating it with local culinary traditions. These traditions are not static in time and space – presenting oneness within multiplicities. The authors and editors lost the opportunity in this context. Potatoes are grown and consumed almost everywhere in the world. But are they cooked and consumed in the same way? The famous aalo-chokha of Bihar is so different from the batata bada of Maharashtra, lentil-based sambhar presents regional variations in southern India, but is so different from the way it is cooked and consumed in north India. Punjabis use rajmah differently from the Kashmiris or Awadhis.

Similarly, when it comes to describing clothing nothing more than the sari remains the focus. What about cultures where the sari is not part of the attire? Can we say that the khasi worn by women in Meghalaya is a variation of the sari? What about the Nagas, the Kashmiris, the Punjabis, and Rajasthanis? Men’s and children’s clothing are not part of the culture of textile and dress-making? What about headgears and ornaments? Even the way women wear saris is so diverse that it calls for some attention. In their zeal to emphasize vegetarianism and sari as India’s food and dressing culture, the authors have thrown the baby with the bath water.

Curiosity, causality, and creativity

Seeing is believing. Keeping facts under wraps is sure to stoke curiosity. Children are curious. They are seldom satisfied with just dry information. In the context of geography – what is where of things. Every time I interact with children I hear searching questions: ‘why’ something is here and why not ‘there’ or why it happened ‘then’ and why now not ‘now’? ‘Why don’t the kings ride on horses anymore?’ ‘Why did Dhritrashtra depend on Sanjaya to tell him about the Kurukshetra war?’ ‘He couldn’t see but could hear FM on the radio?’ The questions could range from genuine curiosity to something fantastic. Children like fantasy, but they also like logical stories.

In the above examples, the textbook neither generates enough curiosity for the information presented nor does it enable children to ask ‘why’: why can’t you make Punjabi parantha or ‘Ma ki daal’? Why do Rajasthani men sport headgear so differently from that of the Sikhs? What makes Hyderabadi Biryani taste different from that of the Avadhi or Bengali variations? The answer is located in the integration and synthesis of cultures due to space relations – a spatial process – THE SPATIALITY, which subsumes both the processes of differentiation and integration akin to the cells in the human body.

[1]W.D. Pattison (1964), "The Four Traditions of Geography” Journal of Geography Vol. 63 no. 5: 211-216.

[2]Browne, Kath, Dhiren Borisa, Mary Gilmartin, and Niharika Banerjea. Social Geographies: the basics (London and New York: Routledge, 2024, p.16)

3Massey, D. For Space. (London and New Delhi: Sage, p.9)

4Soja, Edward W. (1996). Thirdspace: Journeys to Los Angeles and Other Real-and-Imagined Places. Cambridge, MA: Blackwell.

[i] Paul de la Blache (1845-1918), a French geographer and historian, propagator of the idea of possibilism in geography, adopting Lucian Febvre’s critique of the prevailing framework of environmental determinism. Blache’s regional monographs further invigorated the regional concept, and is called the French school of regional geography.

[ii]Alfred Hettner (1851-1941), German geographer who worked extensively on geographical philosophy and methodology, reformulated Immanuel Kant’s concept of chorography (the description of phenomena that are found at the same place) to chorology – a scientific explanation of the total causal relationships of an assemblage of phenomena that are mutually coordinated, but not subordinated, in places).

[iii]Richard Hartshorne (1899-1992), American geographer and author of two well researched books: The Nature of Geography (1939) and Perspectives on the Nature of Geography (1959); a strong advocate of regional geography. Hettner and Hartshorne’s concept of chorology was adopted by a large majority of human geographers from the 1930s until the 1960s.

[iv]Alfred Schaefer published a vigorous criticism of the ‘exceptionalist’ (i.e. each region as unique entities) view of the Kant-Hettner-Hartshorne tradition.  Schaefer argued that no science can thrive on the basis of ‘naïve description’, science searches for laws, and that geographers must set down to seek laws. Hartshorne first published his response to Schaefer’s ‘exceptionalism in geography’ in 1955 and later a book of 10 chapters examining various facets of Schaefer’s criticism.  Unfortunately, Schaefer was not alive to respond to Hartshorne’s defense, but several other geographers indirectly joined the debate.  The so-called ‘quantitative revolution’ in geography began not so much as a consequence of this debate, but more importantly because of geographers’ search for relevance in the changed intellectual and socio-economic contexts of the post World War II.

Sachidanand Sinha is former professor, Centre for the Study of Regional Development, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi. He can be reached at sachi57sinha@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sachidanand Sinha
How well do geography textbooks reflect India’s real diversity? Sachidanand Sinha writes about how the discipline has been evolving.

item.title: Digitalizing geography teaching
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr. N. Saraswathi

The teaching of geography, as an academic discipline, has undergone profound transformations over the past several decades, shaped by pedagogical reforms, technological revolutions, and evolving educational philosophies. From its earliest inclusion in the curricula to its contemporary digital reinvention, geography has served as a conduit through which spatial literacy, environmental awareness, and global citizenship have been cultivated. This article traces the evolution of geography teaching, elucidates the changing roles of educators, and emphasizes the indispensable role of technology in modern pedagogical practices. This exploration highlights the shifts from traditional teaching techniques to the integration of cutting-edge technology and forward-looking approaches that foster critical thinking and environmental stewardship.

Geography in the post-independence syllabus: foundation and early approaches

Following India’s independence, geography had been accorded a prominent position within the school syllabus, with the intention of fostering not only spatial awareness but also a sense of national identity through an understanding of the country’s diverse landscapes and resources. Geography, during the initial years post-independence, had been perceived largely as a subject concerned with the memorization of physical features, political boundaries, and economic resources. The pedagogical approaches employed during this era reflected the educational philosophy of the time – one steeped in the transmission of factual knowledge rather than the cultivation of critical geographical thinking.

The classroom was a space where textbooks reigned supreme and the teacher’s role had primarily been confined to the dissemination of pre-defined content. Learners were expected to passively absorb geographical facts, timelines, and locations. Maps, if available, were static and limited to physical or political representations. While the subject possessed innate potential to inspire curiosity about the Earth and its processes, the pedagogical strategies employed seldom capitalized on this potential. The natural dynamism of geography was subdued by the rigidity of textbook-driven instruction.

The conventional pedagogy: chalk-and-talk

In the subsequent decades, the teaching of geography continued to remain largely teacher-centric. The chalk-and-talk method was employed almost universally, wherein the teacher occupied a central position as the authoritative source of knowledge and students remained passive recipients. The walls of classrooms were adorned with physical and political maps, and the use of atlases was encouraged. However, these tools, while valuable, were only static representations of a world that was constantly changing.

Over-reliance on textbooks and the absence of experiential learning opportunities stifled students' capacity to comprehend the dynamic interplay between physical processes and human activities. Geography, in its essence, is a discipline rooted in spatial interconnectivity, temporal changes, and cultural interpretations – none of which was adequately captured through the teaching strategies of the time.

Field trips, though occasionally organized, were exceptions rather than the norm, and many teachers were constrained by limited resources, institutional inertia, and a rigid curriculum framework. The opportunity to foster geographical imagination – the ability to visualize landscapes, processes, and connections beyond the immediate environment – was inadequately realized. Consequently, geography was perceived by many students as a dry and fact-heavy subject, devoid of dynamism or relevance to their lived experiences.

Technological explosion in the 21st century: a paradigm shift

The dawn of the 21st century heralded an unprecedented explosion of technology that has since revolutionized the teaching and learning of geography. The advent of satellite imagery, Geographic Information Systems (GIS), remote sensing, and interactive digital maps have transformed geography classrooms٭ into immersive spaces of inquisitiveness and discovery. The integration of such technologies has enabled geography to be taught not merely as a subject but as a mode of inquiry – a way of thinking critically about space, place, and scale.

The accessibility of real-time satellite data allows students to observe environmental changes from deforestation in the Amazon to glacial retreat in the Himalayas. Today’s geography classroom has been transformed into a window to the world, with learners able to trace migratory patterns, monitor natural disasters, and analyze urban growth from their desks. Digital platforms such as Google Earth and its intermediaries, have made it possible for students to virtually traverse landscapes they might never physically visit, thereby nurturing a more profound sense of global citizenship and environmental responsibility.

Additionally, the process of creating teaching aids has itself undergone a profound transformation. Teachers are now able to design personalized digital content tailored to the specific needs of their students. Through the use of educational software**, interactive presentations, and simulation tools, abstract geographical concepts – such as plate tectonics, climate change, economic globalization, and parallelism of the Earth’s axis – have been rendered more tangible and comprehensible.

The evolving role of the geography teacher

The role of the geography teacher has undergone a parallel evolution alongside these technological advancements. No longer confined to the role of an authoritative knowledge dispenser, the contemporary geography educator has been re-imagined as a facilitator of inquiry, a curator of information, and a guide to spatial thinking. Teachers are now expected to cultivate not only content knowledge but also technological proficiency, enabling them to leverage digital tools effectively in the service of geography teaching and learning.

This evolution has necessitated a continuous process of professional development, requiring teachers to remain abreast of technological innovations and pedagogical best practices. The geography teacher of today must possess the agility to integrate conventional cartographic skills with digital geospatial technologies, ensuring that students develop a holistic geographical literacy that spans both traditional and contemporary modalities.

Geography is increasingly being taught*** not in isolation but in conjunction with environmental science, economics, history, and political science, reflecting its inherently integrative nature. The geography classroom has thus become a site for interdisciplinary dialogue.

Geography education and digital literacy

In the contemporary educational landscape, digital literacy has emerged as an essential competency, inseparable from effective geography teaching. Students are now expected not only to consume information but to actively create and analyze geography content, transforming them from passive learners into active geographical thinkers****.

This transformation has not been without its challenges. The digital divide – the unequal access to technology and digital literacy – remains a pressing concern, particularly in resource-constrained settings. The reliance on technology in geography teaching has the potential to exacerbate educational inequalities unless deliberate efforts are made to ensure equitable access to digital resources and training.

Sustainability education and the role of geography teaching

As the world grapples with unprecedented environmental challenges, the role of geography teaching in fostering environmental stewardship has assumed paramount importance. Geography educators are uniquely positioned to instil a sense of planetary responsibility by helping students understand the complex interdependencies between human societies and natural systems. Through place-based learning, students are encouraged to engage with their local environments, critically analyze patterns of resource use, and explore pathways toward sustainable development.

The integration of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) into geography curricula further underscores the subject’s relevance in contemporary global discourse. Students are invited to consider how geographical thinking can contribute to solving pressing global issues – from food security and climate resilience to equitable urban development and disaster preparedness.

Charting the future of geography teaching

The trajectory of geography teaching has been one of profound evolution – from rote memorization to inquiry-based learning, from static maps to interactive digital globes, and from teacher-centered instruction to student-centered exploration. This journey reflects not only the changing technological landscape but also a deeper philosophical shift in educational thought – one that values critical thinking, spatial reasoning, and global citizenship.

The future of geography education will be determined not merely by the availability of technological tools but by the willingness of educators to embrace innovation, cultivate curiosity, and foster a deep, ethical engagement with the planet. In an increasingly interconnected and environmentally vulnerable world, geography teaching holds the promise of equipping future generations with the knowledge, skills, and values necessary to navigate and shape a sustainable future.

In sum, geography teaching, when envisioned as a dynamic, inquiry-driven, and technologically enriched endeavour, possesses the potential to transform not only how students understand the world but also how they choose to engage with it. The imperative lies in ensuring that this potential is fully realized through innovative pedagogy, equitable access, and a steadfast commitment to nurturing responsible global citizens.

٭In international schools and geography departments in universities.

**ArcGIS – Advanced mapping and Spatial analysis tool

Google Earth – 3D exploration of landscapes and locations.

QGIS – Open source GIS

Google Maps – Real time navigation and terrain exploration.

Gap minder – Interactive tool for visualizing global trends

Google My Map

***CBSE insists on inter disciplinary approach at all levels. Teachers are given training to approach a topic in any school subject by intertwining, comparing concepts and citing examples in other subjects to help children comprehend.

****Project work at school level on population distribution, literacy level, distribution of industries, and the like are done using data from different sources using the internet and analyzing the same.

Dr. N. Saraswathi is a freelance educationist. With her rich experience of being in the educational field after her teacher training, she has donned many hats as a teacher, the head of an educational institution, an author of books and contributor of articles to reputed magazines, ambassador to the British Council, and a translator. She can be reached at agtichennai@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. N. Saraswathi
Geography teaching is no longer static. Dr. N. Saraswathi writes about how it can be made dynamic, digital, and deeply relevant.

item.title: Resources for teaching geography – they’re everywhere!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Chandra Shekhar Balachandran

To be able to teach geography in interesting, joyous, and empowering ways, we need to develop what I call the geographer’s eye. This is the ability to discern geography everywhere. When this happens, we’d no longer be teaching geography, the subject. Instead, we’d be teaching the discipline of geography – a way of thinking that allows us (and our students) to view the world around us and try to make sense of it all. That is, to understand how geography connects things around us and each other.

Beyond textbooks

The first step towards this view is to get past the textbooks. I am not a big fan of textbooks. They are helpful only to the extent of ensuring that everyone is teaching the same stuff for students to write exams. No more.

Instead of relying on textbooks as the source of geographical learning, we need to be able to show our students geography’s pervasiveness in our lives. Thus, approaching geography as a discipline makes it a life skill, a way of thinking and approaching life itself.

What is geography?

We were all raised with that old definition of geography – ‘geo’ means Earth, ‘graphy’ means study of, therefore ‘geography’ means study of Earth. This is etymologically incorrect – ‘graphy’ means description. Geography has come a long way from being a mere description of Earth; it is a way of understanding how everything on the planet (including us) is interconnected.

When we look at geography this way, we can see it everywhere, in everything.

Most textbooks focus on physical geography, and a little human geography. They present the information as a ‘laundry list’ of things for students to memorize and spew back in the exam. Even the ‘projects’ that students make are hardly better. Teachers tell students what to do for their project, rather than allow them to come up with their own ideas. Teachers are not entirely to blame for this! Given multiple sections of 30-50 students to teach, teachers have little to no time to engage them in any critical thinking to explore project ideas and develop them.

This appears to be an intractable problem. In spite of a system that is not conducive to getting our students to engage with geography (or any other subject/discipline) with a critical mindset, there are teachers who have been working hard to bring home the joy of geographical learning to their students.

This is possible only if the first step is taken beyond the textbook!

Developing the geographer’s eye

This is a vast area to cover. I’ll look at only a few examples and general principles here and leave you with access to a resource that can serve you well, if you are interested.

Example 1: Mental map

Concept: It’s a map that we have in our minds. Our daily route to school is a great example. We don’t consciously say, “I must go 37 steps to the bakery, then turn right, go 49 steps to the light pole, stop, look both ways, cross the road when it is safe to do so, then go 83 steps further till the juice shop, … and reach the school gate.” We do all this subconsciously! How? Because the geographic information is in our memory!

Suggested activity: Ask your students to draw the route they take to school daily (even if they come by school bus or some other vehicle), and write their names on the map. Get them to analyze their maps: Which features were shown more prominently? Why? Are there items that they omitted? Why? Are distances on the map accurate? Are the directions accurate? Is the map oriented to the north? Are there any maps that are similar? If yes, how similar are they? If not, why not? Finally, collect the maps and randomly distribute them among the students. Based on the map they get, can they figure out the starting point (mapmaker’s home) and the route to the ending point (the school)? How tough or easy is it to do this? Why?

Example 2: Relative location

Concept: Location is a fundamental geographic concept. ‘Where is something?’ is the first question of geography. There are two kinds of locations we can use. One is called relative location – the location of things (places) relative to other things (places). We often use this when we give directions. Most of the time, these are not precise.

Suggested activity 1: Ask a student to give directions verbally to another student where they can see each other (i.e., in-person directions). Give a starting point (school?) and an ending point (you can pick something simple and keep making it more complex as you go along).

Suggested activity 2: Ask a student to give verbal directions to someone on an imaginary cell phone. The other person cannot see the one giving directions. This can lead to enormous laughter and fun. Things can get loud and chaotic, but without that, the learning will be lost! So, try to let them go with it, to the extent possible.

Example 3: Latitudes / longitudes

Concept: A second way of identifying location is using latitudes / longitudes. “Latitudes and longitudes are imaginary lines on the surface of the Earth …” is an extremely boring and useless way of starting the topic. It is about the use of a grid. We use grids in so many aspects of daily life. Starting from this, we move to a grid on Earth.

Suggested activity: If possible, take your students to the playground. Don’t share any prior information with them. Carry some chalk pieces (preferably in different colours), a piece of red clay brick, a few flat pebbles or mosaic tile pieces, and a short stick. You may or may not use all these items. No matter!

Once outside, ask the students to form teams and play ‘hopscotch’ (or whatever the local name is). Observe the first thing they do. Ask them to play any other field game you can get them to play. For each of these, observe the first thing they do. They establish boundaries using lines or simple landmarks. For their purposes of playing games, the playground has no order. They impose order on it using boundaries, lines. The moment there are lines, rules appear. Location is important in that game – you win if you reach here, you lose if you go there, etc.

Similarly, we impose order on Earth, in the form of a grid of lines that follow certain rules (here, you can describe the properties of these lines). Once we all accept these rules, we can locate places with precision. How? No two places will have the same location … their latitudes/longitudes will differ. This means every place has a unique location. The beauty of this system is that it lets you make calculations … of distance, area, and so on.

Interactively, connect latitudes/longitudes to cultural phenomena: festivals, software professionals’ health, etc.

Example 4: We create our own geographies

Concept: Spatial production, the process of creating geographies, is a profoundly empowering idea. It emphasizes the power we have to create spaces.

Suggested activity: Begin with a discussion of the physical structure of students’ homes. There will be certain commonalities and differences. Ask them to draw a schematic map (ground plan) of their house.

Ask: Why is that student’s house not your home? What makes your house your home? The distinction here is between house (a merely physical structure) and home (a space based on inter-relationships).

Example 5: Current events reportage

Here is the link (https://tinyurl.com/2y8s9zws) to an article in the Bengaluru-based Deccan Herald newspaper, titled: What I saw at the Maha Kumbh Mela.

Concept: Spatial production. Starting with the fact of this being written by a Kerala Christian reporting on an essentially Hindu festival congregation, this article is brimming with social science (particularly geography) concepts that could be so useful in promoting unifying thinking among our students (and ourselves).

Suggested activity: Give students a copy of the article. Ask them to read it and any other related articles and to view photographs of the event. Then, lead them in a discussion-oriented exploration of geographies of the Maha Kumbh Mela using guiding questions. Here are some examples of guiding questions that occurred to me. You can formulate your own questions.

  • What concepts are you able to discern in this?
  • Which of these could you relate to the textbook that you are using in your classes?
  • What social science questions come to your mind upon reading this article?
  • How might you provoke students into asking thoughtful disciplinary questions that are accompanied by a human(e) understanding of this phenomenon?
  • What interdisciplinary linkages could students and teachers explore using this article?
  • Working within a tight system focused on exams and assessments and grades, how would you open this door to your students?

Suggested resource links:

You can find a large number of resources by attentively browsing through https://tigsindia.com/. Most of these will not directly give you lesson ideas. You will have to read, perhaps multiple sections, to arrive at an idea of how to use the resources in your teaching of geography.

Some specific links that might be useful for the topics in this essay:

  • https://tigsindia.com/home-to-geography/
  • https://tigsindia.com/mirror-mirror-on-the-wall/
  • https://tigsindia.com/wherever-we-go/
  • https://tigsindia.com/transition/
  • https://tigsindia.com/directions/
  • https://tigsindia.com/md-nine/
  • https://tigsindia.com/difference/
  • https://tigsindia.com/those-pesky-lines/

I have deliberately not given more information. How far you go with this will depend on your drive, enthusiasm, ambition, and joy.

Happy exploration!

Dr. Chandra Shekhar Balachandran is Founder, Director of The Institute of Geographical Studies. He can be reached at balachandran@tigsindia.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Chandra Shekhar Balachandran
Geography is a way of seeing the world, writes Dr Chandra Shekhar Balachandran. He shares everyday examples and hands-on activities to develop the ‘geographer’s eye’.

item.title: Take the break!
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
We’re entering that time of year when a certain lethargy takes over. Even as we stare at the piles of uncorrected examination papers, or pack away the last of the notes and materials from the year gone by, or are preparing for the coming onslaught of new minds, there is a sense that it is okay, for a week or two (or more if you’re lucky) when you can let those muscles relax and allow the brain to go a bit mushy. The heat doesn’t help. As I write this, Hyderabad is in the early 40s (degrees Celsius, in case you were wondering if I was on a nostalgia trip). Yes, there are mangoes and sugarcane juice, fresh avakkai and for a few short weeks, the succulent nungu, or ice apple. For some, there is the luxury of long, lazy summer days spent at home. Clearly, a recipe for inertia.

Lean into it, I’d say, and keep the guilt at bay. Easier said than done, of course.

There are still the many demands of life, professional and personal, as well as the need to catch up on all the things we had put off until we could find the time: the time that we should have at our disposal, now that we are officially on a break. But then, that is the nature of life; too much to do, and too little time to do it in.

Which brings me to the theme of our summer issue – geography. In the 17-odd years that Teacher Plus has been publishing subject-themed issues, we have covered geography before, but as with any theme we pick up, we find that there is always much more to be said, and not enough pages to say it in. So, we’ve come back to subjects and revisited them using new lenses, drawing on sensibilities of the times and bringing in new voices. As with every subject, there is also new knowledge that shifts what and how we teach. As a discipline, geography stands at the intersection of the physical or natural and the human sciences, and its study thus draws from physics and materials science as much as from sociology and political science. The new digital tools that we have at our disposal today offer the possibility of a much more intimate interaction with a range of geographic and geophysical phenomena. An understanding and appreciation of geography can inform how we think about and act on issues of climate change.

Through the month, we will bring you a range of articles on geography teaching – some inviting you to re-think the boundaries of the discipline, and others sharing ideas that have worked well in their classrooms. As always, there are unexpected lessons that emerge when one is attentive, in (perhaps) unlikely places – for instance, Sheel describes how a spiritual or religious event like the Mahakumbh mela presents an unmissable “concomitance of geography, history and culture”. On the other hand, Chandra Shekhar Balachandran exhorts us to see that geography is everywhere – and maybe in everything, all at once! But more on these and the other pieces that make up the issue in our regular weekly newsletters.

Long-time readers of the magazine will know that the subject special was earlier published as a double issue over May and June – a nice thick package of information and ideas from a wide range of contributors. With our digital avatar, the special issue unfolds over one month instead of two, but with a similar amount of content, as well as diversity. The geography teachers among you will have much to take into your classes in the new year, but there’s plenty in the issue that relates to other subjects as well, such as Tapasya Saha’s take on bringing current events into teaching, or Aditya Deshbandhu’s exhortation to make good use of video games for learning.

But even as we keep plying you with material through the year, we are conscious of the fact that the summer heat is not something we should take lightly, and that we also need to make good use of the break to recharge our batteries. How do you do that? We’d love to hear from you!
item.excerpt:encoded: We’re entering that time of year when a certain lethargy takes over. Even as we stare at the piles of uncorrected examination papers, or pack away the last of the notes and materials from the year gone by, or are preparing for the coming onslaught of new minds, there is a sense that it is okay, for a week or two (or more if you’re lucky) when you can let those muscles relax and allow the brain to go a bit mushy.

item.title: Enhancing student engagement through technology-rich learning environments
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Kirti Sharma

EdTech implementation has transformed pedagogy and enabled educational institutions to embrace a new era of accessibility, innovation, and critical thinking. Therefore, forward-thinking schools are increasingly embracing digital technologies to prepare the next generation for a digital future. A K12 Digital Transformation-2023 Survey by Holon IQ shows that 76% of schools prioritize technology integration into education. This realization is metamorphosizing classrooms into innovation hubs.


EdTech revolution in India

EdTech is fundamental in bridging educational gaps, specifically in India's rural and underserved areas. Furthermore, GoI's New Education Policy 2020 supports this drive by improving the educational system's quality, integrity, and transparency. This initiative also complements India's education sector's Sustainable Development Goal 4, prioritizing inclusive and top-notch education for all.

India's EdTech sector will likely reach $29 billion by 2030 and grow at a CAGR of 25.8%. This indicates the growing influence of digital tools in education. EdTech tools will gain more traction in the future, driven by low-cost digital tools and public-private collaborations; nevertheless, this growth must be accompanied by robust regulations on ethical problems like privacy and equity so that all learners get the most from technology.

The role of personalization

Personalized learning systems and artificial intelligence (AI) are two of the most ground-breaking EdTech breakthroughs. AI customizes educational experiences to suit individual student needs and encourages a growth mindset among them. It also offers personalized lessons based on the real-time analysis of students' overall performances.

Also, virtual and augmented reality have turned classrooms into experiential learning centers. For instance, history students can now virtually visit an ancient civilization while biology students can perform virtual anatomy dissection. These EdTech tools make education engaging and impactful by bringing subjects to life. Recent industry reports claim that 50% of Indian schools will adopt virtual and augmented reality in three years.

EdTech tools also make students job-ready by instilling the traits of critical thinking, collaboration, and adaptability in them through interactive and collaborative platforms. For instance, group projects, enabled by cloud-based tools, enhance teamwork besides preparing students for the collaborative nature of modern workplaces. Additionally, EdTech emphasizes STEM education, soft skills development, and career-oriented programs to make students job-ready.

According to the World Economic Forum, 150 million new technology jobs will be created globally over the next few years. Additionally, by 2030, 77% of jobs will require digital skills. However, businesses are already facing a global skills shortage, with only 33% of tech roles currently filled by qualified workers. Thus, students must receive digital education to equip themselves with the technical skills needed to thrive in the future job market.


EdTech as social catalyst

EdTech tools have the potential to transform students more than academics do. It makes them digitally literate in an increasingly technology-dependent world. Modern classrooms are also emerging as safe spaces in which children learn about online etiquette, privacy, and the consequences of their digital footprints. EdTech also helps build international connections. Virtual classrooms allow students to work on projects with fellow students from other countries. Such collaborations open their minds and promote intercultural learning, preparing them for a global workforce that requires cross-cultural communication.


Changing role of teachers

EdTech is also helping teachers perform better. It helps them create meaningful learning experiences by leveraging their pedagogical strengths, customizing instruction, and engaging learners. A 2022 survey by the India EdTech Consortium shows that 82% of teachers believe technology has enhanced their teaching skills. With digital tools, teachers can become mentors to help their students develop critical skills. Additionally, professional development programs emphasizing technology integration make teachers into change agents.

Challenges and the way forward

Schools willing to adopt EdTech tools must invest in infrastructure, teacher training, and digital resources while considering data security issues. Bridging the rural-urban digital divide is another priority, especially in less connected and resource-constrained areas. Ethical concerns about data privacy, bias in algorithms, and equitable access also require strict regulatory frameworks. With AI-driven tools gathering vast amounts of data to personalize learning, strong policies are necessary to ensure this data is used responsibly.

Public-private partnerships and targeted investments in digital infrastructure can overcome these challenges. Educators and policymakers must also collaborate to build an environment where technology can enhance learning without compromising privacy.

Education is evolving to become more accessible and inclusive, driven by technology integration. Advanced EdTech tools enable institutions to provide adaptive and collaborative learning experiences, ensuring broader access for diverse learners. 

Kirti Sharma is Co-Ceo, Crimson schools.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kirti Sharma
Technology in education is here to stay. So instead of denying it or finding flaws, let us embrace it.

item.title: Making economics accessible
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

What is this book about?

“This book tells the stories of Nilou and her friends, a group of children and teens, and the adults who live with them. These children live in a village, one which could be in India or Kenya or Vietnam or elsewhere, and we see them deal with some ordinary and not-so-ordinary challenges.” Stories from “a world both very different and yet intensely familiar”.

These stories are connected by a set of common characters, and of course, the setting. Education, health, urban migration, elections, fear, beliefs, food, greenery, livelihood, government, and gender are the topics they touch upon. The issues they highlight are universal.

This is also a book written during or just after the COVID pandemic.

What works in the book?

Illustrations. This book is not text heavy. Its illustrations and fonts are different from those one comes across in Indian children’s books these days. The book size and production quality are friendly; they make flipping pages easy. Together, they keep the reader glued to the stories. My favourite page is the one on education (Nilou – Skips school) where children read “Ar-chi-mede The-o-rem” as “Archie made tea and rum”. It would be fun to discuss the illustrations with the children as also to get them to create fresh stories with these characters.

Text. The format of the book - personal narrative juxtaposed with fictional stories – works well. The personal narrative is peppered with lines that one can reread, “Something incredible happens when people come together and draw a map: they are able to remember everything they have in their village, and also when it was built, when it was repaired or improved, whether it is still in use, etc.”

Questions. The author hopes that the stories encourage readers to “ask many new questions”. The questions, especially those in the story on urban migration (Neso and Najy - Not afraid of the big city), make one wonder!

What does not work?

Approach. These stories present a world that is very simple and nice -- government servants are all helpful and people are taking up positive and good actions like “recycling” and “making nice things for all”. A world that is black and white. A world more from the times of Amar Chitra Katha of a couple of decades ago than the Eklavya and Ektara books of today. The book does not respect the intelligence of children.

Cliché. In the story on health (Afia is feeling sick) the pharmacist has “fake pills” and the villagers “love antibiotics”. The rich person is fat and a villain while “scientist” and “best way” go together. This book ends up underscoring the cliché. They reminded me of Stephen Fry’s well-known take on cliché, “It is a cliché that most clichés are true, but then like most clichés, that cliché is untrue.’

Authors’ lens. Across the stories, the solutions always come from the city – be it the nurse who got free bednets (health), or the government officer who makes payments for trees (greenery), or the supermarket (vegetables) or even the “ideas for activities” and microbanker (livelihood). And, for some reason, the villagers are always being challenged! Lines like this too don’t exactly help, “Have you ever wondered why some people continue to live in faraway villages when life becomes very difficult there?” Is life easier in the cities – especially for those who do not have surplus? In today’s times when increasingly the urban – rural divide is getting blurred what are “faraway villages”? And, “faraway” from whom?

Lack of clarity. Neither the title of the book nor the age group the book caters to are clear. On the one hand, the book talks of too many issues, on the other, it has little to offer that is fresh. The price of the book will make it out of bounds for those it talks about. And, could the title have been more apt?

Nimesh Ved loves walking, cycling, and is often seen amidst books. He enjoys putting his thoughts in words. He blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/. He can be reached at nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
Economics as a subject is introduced to children in middle school, but it is possible for children to engage with the subject even at younger ages through books like Poor Economics for Kids. The book has its pros and cons. Read the review to find out if your needs will be met.

item.title: The urgency of small leaps
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anand Krishnaswamy

Go as far as your feet can take you. Or just cross the road. Take the biggest leap you ever have. Or maybe a small one. Now look, really look, at what lies beneath your feet, or inhale that smell wafting gently towards your nostrils, or observe the sunshine dancing through the leaves or between the grills on your window. When was the last time you actually studied these?

There is an urgent need for geography teachers to study the works of Tristan Gooley. He makes a passionate call to reading the proximal elements that form our surroundings. I agree with him that students need to be more conversant with their immediate world before venturing into fjords and geysers, especially if the latter phenomena do not exist within a hundred miles of their dwelling. This need is not a romantic call, but an essential competence that is usually lacking. The lack is not merely in the naming, but in the sensitivity to discern and study the many variations and nuances of our surroundings.

Tristan Gooley's works explore the forgotten art of reading natural signs, offering a gateway to a deeper understanding of our environment. His books weave practical knowledge with philosophical insights about our connection to nature.

Gooley emphasizes that natural navigation isn't merely about finding direction, but developing a relationship with our surroundings. He teaches readers to notice subtle clues – from wind-shaped trees to cloud formations – that reveal stories about our environment.

His approach transforms ordinary walks into journeys of discovery, where puddles, plants, and prevailing winds become meaningful indicators rather than background scenery. Many of these do not find technical names in textbooks but are vital compasses of true navigators, especially indigenous people. Gooley reminds us that this knowledge transcends mere utility. By incorporating these natural literacy skills, geography teachers can cultivate in students not merely the ability to study the landscape, but to read it with comprehension and appreciation.

He provides us with a simple activity that teachers can explore on a field trip (which, I am aware, most schools do not provide). He calls it “Getting SORTED” where SORTED stands for:

S – Shape

O – Overall character

R – Routes

T – Tracks

E – Edges

D – Detail

This activity involves sketching a landscape. This is one of the general ways of reading the landscape more effectively. Getting SORTED is basically a more specific approach to acquiring this skill.

These six steps will help the student notice many more useful clues than if they try to take in everything simultaneously. Each step could take up a whole book, but the aim is to show you the method. If you don’t learn how to have a bit of fun with this, it isn’t likely to stick with you for long. That said, there are two stages: the first part – SOR – is about getting a good picture of your surroundings; the second – TED – is about finding the clues within, which is where the fun really starts. I would encourage the reader to explore this in Gooley’s book – The Lost Art of Reading Nature’s Signs. His other books include How to Read Nature, How to Read Water, The Natural Navigator, and The Secret World of Weather.

A summarized list of action items for the geography teacher would include:

  1. Field observation journal: Have students maintain observation logs, documenting natural patterns they notice during regular outdoor sessions.
  2. Local navigation challenge: Create campus-based activities where students navigate using only natural signs.
  3. Weather prediction projects: Implement weekly forecasting exercises using cloud formations and atmospheric indicators from The Secret World of Weather.
  4. Water reading workshops: Organize stream or pond visits to observe flow patterns, ripples, and reflections as described in How to Read Water.
  5. Tree biography assignment: Have students "interview" a tree using Gooley's techniques, documenting its life story through growth patterns, orientation, and adaptations.
  6. Cross-curricular integration: Connect natural navigation to history, discussing how these techniques shaped exploration and settlement patterns.

In case the reader wonders why the author recommends these books and the ideas of Gooley, then here is my motivation – to encourage a place-based approach to the study of geography. David Sobel was the person who introduced me to this approach decades ago and it has appealed to my every sensibility since then. Searching online might provide many resources to understand what Place Based Pedagogy or Education is and here is one such resource: https://www.gettingsmart.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/What-is-Place-Based-Education-and-Why-Does-it-Matter-4.pdf.

I would particularly like to draw the attention of the reader to a research paper titled: Place-based education and pre-service teachers: a case study from India (http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/13603116.2013.855265). This is a case study on place-based education involving Australian pre-service teachers and children in a Delhi slum. The pre-service teachers engaged in collaborative planning and team teaching, focusing on the interests and needs of the Kalkaji children. Key activities included a photography project, family portraits and stories collection, a dance and drama performance, and discussions on popular culture and politics. The methodology emphasized cross-cultural awareness, building equitable relationships, and honouring the children's existing knowledge to foster active communication and engagement. The teacher candidates built personal connections with the Kalkaji girls by hosting an afternoon tea where they shared Australian lamington cakes and engaged in informal conversations. This setting allowed for personal storytelling and the sharing of experiences, fostering a comfortable environment for the girls to express themselves. Over time, as the girls rehearsed their performance, their confidence grew, leading to increased collaboration and idea-sharing among them. The teaching team faced challenges due to a lack of information about the students, available resources, and the teaching environment in Kalkaji. With only a single one-hour lesson each day, ensuring that learning goals were achievable became a significant concern. Additionally, the transient nature of short-term volunteer work raised questions about the sustainability and relevance of their teaching efforts. The teacher candidates undertook various projects, including a photographic project where children captured images of their lives, family portraits and stories collection, and a dance and drama performance based on Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet. They also engaged in discussions about popular culture, such as understanding song lyrics to improve English language skills. These projects were designed to be relevant to the children's interests and experiences, fostering active participation and learning. Essential to the success of the place-based education initiative in Kalkaji was focused preparation that emphasized cross-cultural awareness, sensitivity, and a rejection of deficit views regarding children and families living in slums. Additionally, understanding the socio-political complexities of India and building equitable relationships with the children and NGO staff were crucial. This approach ensured that the teaching was relevant, respectful, and responsive to the local community's needs and resources. It highlights how the unique attributes of a place can connect self with community and serve as a resource for learning. The teaching initiatives aimed to embed education within the local context of Kalkaji, reflecting the significance of geography in the educational experience.

The outcomes of the teaching initiative included enhanced student engagement, improved performance, and the development of meaningful connections between the teacher candidates and the Kalkaji children. By focusing on real-world learning and community involvement, the projects fostered a sense of relevance and ownership among the students, aligning with the principles of place-based education. This approach not only bridged the gap between school and community but also empowered students by valuing their existing knowledge and experiences, ultimately leading to more effective and inclusive learning.

Similar interventions conducted in Turkey and other countries have shown that place-based pedagogies and approaches to teaching social sciences, especially geography tends to improve the academic performance and awareness about their surroundings as well as reduce any deficit mindsets about what their locality had to offer. This increased sensitivity is also likely to fuel curiosity about spaces far and beyond their physical reach, helping them build mental and visual models much better than when restricted to textbooks and 2D sketches.

Place-based education transforms learning by anchoring knowledge in local ecosystems, creating authentic connections between students and their surroundings. In India – a land where tropical rainforests meet alpine meadows, where mighty rivers carve ancient valleys, and where coastal mangroves transition to desert landscapes – this approach offers unparalleled educational potential.

As Tristan Gooley reminds us, "The land around us is full of signs, and we can learn to read it as easily as we might read a book." India's geographical tapestry provides an exceptional text for this natural literacy. From the Western Ghats' biodiversity hotspot to the Himalayan highlands, from Kerala's backwaters to Rajasthan's arid expanses, each landscape tells distinct stories through its flora, fauna, weather patterns, and geological features. Each town and city has its own unique elements to study and connect with, which can be folded into any chapter of the textbook. The NEP has also gone ahead to recognize the importance of approaching learning in such a manner.

Place-based education in India can honour traditional ecological knowledge while developing modern scientific understanding. For centuries, indigenous communities have read monsoon signs, navigated by stars, and identified medicinal plants – knowledge systems increasingly vital as climate change accelerates. By learning directly from local environments, students develop not just geographical knowledge but cultural context and environmental stewardship.

Gooley's approach reminds us that in our technology-saturated world, reconnecting with natural surroundings cultivates observation skills, patience, and presence. Students who learn to read the direction of flowing water, interpret cloud formations, or recognize bird migration patterns develop multisensory intelligence that transcends textbook learning.

The diverse landscapes of India offer ideal laboratories for this immersive education. By stepping outside classroom walls and into local watersheds, forests, and urban green spaces, educators can nurture the next generation of environmental stewards who understand that the journey is always more meaningful than the destination.

Other references:

  • Adoption of Place-based Learning in a Rural Primary Government School in India
  • The Impact of Place Based Education Approach on Student Achievement in Social Studies
  • https://blog.education.nationalgeographic.org/2020/07/30/place-based-learning-exploring-and-unpacking-the-world-from-anywhere/
  • https://www.placeness.com/place-in-the-polycrisis/

Anand Krishnaswamy is a computer scientist turned educator (self-taught). He is an author, speaker, educational consultant and coach and has worked with several schools and teachers as well as coached school leaders. In the past he has led the academic practice at NGO schools in India. He can be reached at anand.krishnaswamy@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anand Krishnaswamy
Before fjords and volcanoes, geography is about the tree outside your window. Anand Krishnaswamy writes about how teachers can use the familiar, and let nature be students’ first textbook.

item.title: Learning geography firsthand: making a case for experiential field visits
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kavita Anand

As an ‘arts’ student in my higher secondary school in Delhi, I studied geography in some depth because my school did not have other choices. Thanks to that enforced option, I find it much easier to understand and have a perspective on critical issues around climate change, migration patterns, the depletion of the Earth’s resources and the impact of these on all life on the planet and its future. This article is for the teachers of geography in primary and secondary schools who have the crucially important job of enabling every student to develop a deep and enduring understanding of and love for our planet, an appreciation of its incredible diversity and uniqueness and the need for each one of us to be a part of its wellbeing story.

Fifty years after primary school, standing in my garden in England at different times of the year, I observed the sun rising and travelling across the sky. In winter it sets sometimes as early as 4 pm and in summer it looks unwilling to set even at 9 pm. It was then that I realized what I had only read in textbooks as a child. The rotation and revolution of the Earth around the sun, the tilt of the Earth, the change in the duration of the day, the seasons, and the arc of travel of the sun came alive in ways they never had in India. My textbook and the models I had drawn when I was 12 without any real understanding finally made complete sense!

As a student, I encountered this new fascinating subject called geography through which I learned that I live on a planet called Earth, a very large sphere, that comprises of land and sea, that has seven continents, and that is one of nine planets that go around a sun. It took a leap of faith for me, and probably does for all primary school students who are in the concrete operational stage of human development in which abstract reasoning is a challenge, to believe their teachers when told that we live on a large ball and that it has a mysterious something called gravity that ensures we do not fall off! Thanks to the pictures and representations in the textbooks, I remember wondering as a child with a rather vivid imagination, what it would take to live in the southern hemisphere and whether the Australians and New Zealanders realized that they were always upside down in space!

Luckily for me I had a passionate geography teacher who tried hard to go beyond the textbook and use pictures, maps, 3D models and innovative teaching-learning material to explain abstract concepts. All passionate teachers of geography ask their students to use the atlas, create their own models, and discuss places and landforms they have visited in their holidays. A small number of these teachers take their students on field visits to local parks, hills, or rivers to experience vegetation, landforms, markets, and people firsthand. I was one of them.

Starting with what we eat

When developing the curriculum for Shishuvan, the school I set up in Mumbai and ran for over a decade, geography and field visits played a great part in all our learning experiences that were designed. We used every opportunity for students to understand how they lived, ate, and clothed themselves in a way that went beyond theoretical knowledge. In the fourth grade, for example, all students were taken through the process of growing rice, over several months, in a field in Dahanu, Maharashtra. They would work with farmers in one field, clearing the field, preparing the soil, and then sowing the seeds. Weeks later, they would return to transplant the saplings in soft squelching mud, enjoying the novelty and also appreciating the back-breaking labour of farmers. When it was time to cut the stalks, thresh the grains, and dry them in the sun, the students would also visit the mill that separated the grain from the chaff and take a bag of rice back to school to cook and eat the grains of their labour. It creates a completely different sense of time, purpose, productivity, and relationship with land when students have real-time experience rather than just reading and remembering that “Rice is a kharif crop that thrives in high temperatures”.

Getting to know your locale

Students’ first experience of map making began by drawing their neighbourhood and the area around the school. While we used Google Earth to enable a 3D aerial view, we always added to the visual by enabling field visits for every cohort. No video can replace the actual experience of hearing, smelling, touching, and tasting that makes students value their learning. Taking advantage of the city and its location, visits were made to Dharavi – the largest slum in Asia; to Dadar beach for a clean up of the plastic waste on the shore; to the Sewri Creek to understand the migration of flamingoes from Central Asia and Africa because of the mangroves and mudflats that create a desirable habitat; to the Borivili national park to see tigers who sadly showed up to be fed when the bus driver blew the horn; and to BKC Garden, developed on a landfill as part of an urban renewal project that enabled an interesting conversation around waste and environmental reclamation. This is perfectly doable by all teachers anywhere in the world. In order to make it safe and feasible with 108 students in every cohort, we organized parent volunteers and took the support staff along. We also returned to school to have discussions on what we had seen, how it related with what the students were studying and what the students could do to express their learning through models, poems, songs, dances, drawings, and debates.

Going outside your comfort zone

In middle school, the field visits had a clear purpose of taking students out of their immediate experience, to other Indian states. Taking the 5th graders to the Rann of Kutch, for instance, enabled for each student, a powerful experience of isolation caused by the almost unnatural quality of silence, the colour and flatness of the land, and the sense of insignificance when standing in an expansive space where the sky meets the earth at an extreme distance unbroken by any vegetation. It was designed to encourage a sense of awe and wonder when encountering a part of the planet that is not inviting or easy. The visit to the Rann was part of a week-long immersion in Kutch and Saurashtra to understand the interdependence between people and their ecosystem. Deep understanding is achieved when students’ senses, emotions, and intellects align – when it’s not just reading a paragraph on the ecosystem’s importance. In every cohort, the connections were made in a wonderful non-linear manner when students understood how their own and other people’s daily life was connected with the environment. A part of the visit that was personally important was to a women’s cooperative that wove the cloth for the school uniform worn daily by the students. Speaking with the weavers and understanding how the uniform enables their livelihood and wellbeing, brought alive the connection between the cotton that grows in Gujarat’s semi-arid climate and the flourishing textile industry that enables the women to live in dignity.

Travel as learning

The mode of travel is equally important. Most of our field visits were undertaken on the bus and train – a great way to create a direct connection to the landscapes passing by that give a real sense of distance and geography. More importantly, we insisted on second class travel in noisy, crowded compartments shared with people and visited by vendors and entertainers that students may never have otherwise met. This made the field visits a rich lesson in society, culture, diversity, social awareness, and real-world understanding. The open windows that let in air, dust, heat, cold and rain, the different food at every stop, the gradual transition between regions, made the train journey itself a live lesson in physical and human geography. Field visits that involved hiking to a Shivaji fort were a perfect way to connect geography with history, science, and language. The structure of the fort enabled many discussions about the usefulness of hill top forts that enabled the army to sight their enemy at a distance, keep their people safe, and use their knowledge of science to build the structure and weaponry. The 8th grade visit to Chennai and Puducherry always included an awe-inspiring beginning of the day on Marina beach, to watch the sun come out of the ocean rather than the daily experience of watching it set in the Arabian sea in Mumbai. Naturally, the idea of the East and West became an experiential geography lesson.

Connecting current events with global issues

Today climate change is a reality that is seen in increasing reports of natural disasters in India and the rest of the world. One of my first memories of experientially understanding the impact of geographical change on human beings was during my visit to Harsud in Madhya Pradesh at the turn of the century just before it was submerged by the rising waters of the Narmada due to the development of the dam. Harsud that day was a forlorn ghost town, unwillingly abandoned, with eerily empty houses echoing with grieving, dragging feet. The forced migration and loss of homeland in which they had lived for centuries, without control on the timing, circumstances, or choice of leaving home and livelihood meant a complete disruption to the lives of the inhabitants and the culture of the community. I stood on the street, imagining the water flooding the village and then covering the houses, fields, shops, and temples, till they couldn’t be seen from the shore.
