I wondered if divers guided by their flippers through the underwater town years later would still be able to hear the stories of the displaced residents and make the link between global geo-political issues that plague our planet in the current century. Speaking with students about my experience as a teacher, made human geography real for them. I cannot imagine a more important subject than geography in enabling a deep emotional connection with and understanding of our beautiful rich and diverse planet, and its incredible vegetation, animal life, geology, and people.

Kavita Anand is an educator and social entrepreneur who treats her life experiences as her classroom. She can be reached at kavita.anand@adhyayan.asia.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kavita Anand
From growing rice to watching the sunrise on Marina beach, Kavita Anand writes about real-world encounters that can make geography meaningful, memorable, and deeply human.

item.title: Where stones speak: exploring geography through heritage sites
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Radhika Gupta

Geography has always fascinated me. As a student, my mind would construe it as a subject that dealt with another world…a world far, far away! I would imagine it in my head through intricate maps and abstract concepts, like landforms that I had read about only in books, crops that I had never seen or eaten, and soils described in various colours – so different from the endless brown around me. Back then, the world wasn’t as open a place as it is today. Our exposure was limited. Summer vacation simply meant a trip to our grandparents’ house. Travelling to distant lands wasn’t a norm. The internet did not exist, nor were there any educational videos. We mostly banked on our imaginations as a gateway to the unknown. From lava flows to high tides, I remember piecing together little bits of geographical knowledge collected from various film scenes, photographs, or anecdotes of privileged relatives, and imagining it all…and yes, it was fun!

However, this reliance on imagination belongs to a bygone era. Today, with technology at our fingertips, the world unfolds before us in an instant. Immersive experiences created by Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) have added a new dimension to learning. Special effects in films and documentaries, high resolution camera pictures, detailed blogs, various geographical websites, and social media sites shape our experiences without us having to physically go anywhere. This lends some perspective to the contents of the geography textbook as well.

Notwithstanding the marvels of technology, I admit to being an old-schooler at heart, for I still believe in the magic of firsthand experiences, especially in the social sciences. It’s all about looking around and absorbing the environment. But how do you bring that experience into the classroom? How do you make students see the connection between the land and its historical past, and feel the weight of history in the stone walls and winding streets of an ancient city?

Back in 2015, I was struggling with a chapter on landforms with my grade V students. Living in Delhi, they could not fathom what a plateau looked like, or what the challenges of a desert could be. Simultaneously, I had also started teaching them Mughal history. The textbook wasn’t comprehensive enough, nor was it as pictorial as we would have liked it to be. My students would listen to me obediently, but I could see that they were relying on their imagination, the way I did in my school days. Frustration bubbled inside me. I wanted to make their learning experience much better than what mine had been. It was this thought that led to an experiment, one that did not begin in the classroom.

As I sat in the car for a family trip to Rajasthan, thoughts about my classroom wheeled around my mind. What if the forts, lakes, and streets of Jaipur turned into an extended classroom, bringing history and geography to life? I sensed an opportunity. Standing atop the golden ramparts of Amer Fort, I could almost hear the whispers of history being carried by the desert wind. The relentless sun beat down on the ochre landscape, where camels must have plodded along ancient trade routes once bustling with merchants, warriors, and royal caravans. The storyteller in me was already collecting pieces of a lesson yet to be taught. As I wandered through the pink-hued streets of Jaipur, and later the shimmering lakes of Udaipur, I wasn’t just a traveller on a vacation – I was a teacher on a mission. Every fort I climbed, every stepwell I peered into, every intricate archway I traced with my fingers, I saw it all through the eyes of my students. Could they imagine how the dry, rugged hills of Jaipur had shaped the mighty forts? Would they see how the glistening waters of Udaipur weren’t just beautiful, but a lifeline in an arid land?

So, I did what any curious teacher would do. I filmed everything. I narrated stories as I stood atop the Nahargarh Fort, panned my camera over the symmetrical courtyards of City Palace, and captured the way the golden light danced on the waters of Fateh Sagar Lake. It was Dussehra, so I was lucky to capture the festivities too. My family humoured me as I turned our holiday into a living textbook, collecting moments instead of souvenirs.

Back in the classroom, my students weren’t flipping through textbook pages any more. In fact, they were travelling with me through my visual experience. Each day, I unveiled a new piece of my journey. We discussed Jaipur’s strategic city planning, the defensive genius of Amer Fort, Udaipur’s floating palaces that defied the desert, and so on. With every video, every picture, every animated retelling, I watched their curiosity spark. History wasn’t just about emperors and battles after all, and geography wasn’t just about abstract maps and landforms. Here, in the grand forts and glistening lakes of Rajasthan, they saw how the land had shaped history, and how history, in turn, had adapted to the land.

While this experiment brought the forts and lakes of Rajasthan into the classroom with aplomb, it still didn’t match up to the firsthand experience of standing beneath the towering gates of Jaigarh Fort, the feeling of touching the rough stones under your fingers, or watching the evening sun turn Lake Pichola into molten gold. This is the power of place we’ve all experienced, the moments when we’re deeply immersed in a real-life location, making meaningful and relatable connections. The knowledge gained in such moments is organic and visceral. Yet, educational experiences that leverage the power of place still remain an exception, and not the rule.

That’s when I stumbled upon Place Based Learning, a pedagogy that felt like it had put words to what I had been doing intuitively. Widely practiced in the West, this approach anchors learning in the local environment, using communities, heritage sites, and natural landscapes as ‘living classrooms’. The idea is simple yet powerful: instead of isolating subjects within the four walls of a classroom, students engage directly with their surroundings, seeing history in the stones of a fort, geography in the curves of a river, and culture in the rhythms of a bustling market square.

Cut to 2023, I was pleasantly surprised to see this approach being depicted in the Bollywood film Bawaal, where a history teacher, struggling to teach his students effectively, embarks on a journey across Europe to teach them about World War II through his own eyes. From Anne Frank’s House to the Nazi concentration camps, he turns history into a lived experience, making his students feel its weight beyond textbook pages. While being dramatized for cinema, the film echoes a fundamental truth – when learning is rooted in a place, it transforms into something tangible, and becomes unforgettable.

However, at the ground level in India, this method has not yet gained mainstream acceptance. Our system remains tethered to textbooks and examinations, leaving little room for experiential learning rooted in a place as reality poses its own set of challenges. Not every school has the means to send students on immersive journeys due to lack of resources, funding issues, safety concerns, and logistical constraints that often make travel a privilege rather than a pedagogical tool. Even when schools organize trips, balancing engagement with preservation is essential. Students should be encouraged to explore a place, but they must do so responsibly to protect its fragile past. And then comes the greatest challenge of all, which entails fitting this kind of learning into the rigid framework of structured curriculums. How do we justify time away from the classroom when every lesson is mapped against exams and school timelines?

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 envisions a transformative shift towards multidisciplinary education, aiming to cultivate well-rounded individuals equipped with diverse skill sets. It also emphasizes experiential learning, which advocates hands-on experiences that deepen students' understanding of their environment and culture. Backed by this, we can reorient our pedagogies by bringing experiential learning into our everyday teaching, whether it is through field visits, digital explorations, or hands-on projects. To address challenges such as resource constraints and accessibility, schools can explore partnerships with local organizations, virtual field trips, or community-based projects. Instead of assigning lengthy holiday homework, schools should encourage parents to take their children to heritage sites twice a year, whether near or far, as per the financial resources available with them. Students can return from the vacations with photographs and stories to share with their teachers and classmates. This exchange of experiences will spark curiosity, foster discussion, and make learning more personal and meaningful.

Teaching geography through heritage sites allows students to witness the dynamic interplay between history, ecology, and urban planning. Take for instance, the grand temples of South India, where geography and architecture merge seamlessly. The Brihadeeswara Temple in Thanjavur stands tall as a testament to Chola engineering and to the region’s stable alluvial plains that supported a flourishing agrarian economy. The Meenakshi Temple in Madurai, with its intricate water management system, speaks of how ancient societies adapted to the monsoon-fed Vaigai River. Hampi, with its boulder-strewn landscape, was the capital of the Vijayanagar Empire. It was a city carefully built around natural rock formations and water reservoirs, ensuring both its military defence and life sustainability. The religious ghats of Varanasi, nestled along the Ganges, are a lesson in how geography influences culture and settlement patterns. Even city planning, often viewed as a modern discipline, has deep geographical roots. Jaipur, one of India’s earliest planned cities, was designed with remarkable precision. Its streets are laid out based on wind direction to ensure ventilation, and its buildings are aligned with the sun’s movement for natural cooling.

When subjects are taught in isolation, students miss these fascinating interconnections. But when they are interlinked, they then become stories of adaptation, survival, and innovation, embedded in the various heritage sites that surround us. Education should effectively weave connections between subjects. The walls of a classroom can only take us so far. True learning begins when we step outside, with our eyes wide open to the world around us. In the current education system, we are drowning in information but are starved for knowledge. A textbook can give us facts, maps, and definitions, but a whole new world of understanding unfolds only when we move beyond the confines of its pages. Gadgets are causing sensory overload in students. What will truly stay with them is what they see, touch, feel, hear, and experience firsthand. This personal connection will change the way they see the world. Concluding in the words of Barack Obama, “The study of geography is about more than just memorizing places on a map. It's about understanding the complexity of our world, appreciating the diversity of cultures that exists across continents. And in the end, it's about using all that knowledge to help bridge divides and bring people together.”

References

  • https://www.education.gov.in/nep/multidisciplinary-holistic-education
  • https://examrobot.com/content/who-among-following-rulers-vijayanagara-empire-constructed-large-dam-across-tungabhadra
  • https://www.deccanherald.com/india/karnataka/the-path-of-water-in-vijayanagara-1052896.html
  • https://mybrightwheel.com/blog/field-trip-ideas-for-preschoolers
  • https://research.com/education/the-educational-value-of-field-trips
  • https://www.thenatureinstitute.org/field-trips/
  • https://www.nationalgeographic.org/society/experiential-learning/
  • https://www.weareteachers.com/best-virtual-field-trips/
  • https://www.gettingsmart.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/What-is-Place-Based-Education-and-Why-Does-it-Matter-4.pdf
  • https://www.uwinnipeg.ca/experiential-learning/docs/Place-Based%20Learning.pdf
  • https://www.promiseofplace.org/
  • https://www.placeness.com/place-in-the-polycrisis/

Radhika Gupta is Education Advisor (Senior Wing), St Kabir Public School, Chandigarh. She can be reached at rads.gupta@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Radhika Gupta
A family trip to Rajasthan inspires Radhika Gupta to film the sights and sounds to bring alive Jaipur’s forts, lakes and streets for her students. Doing so introduced her to place-based learning.

item.title: Contextualizing geography education in Ladakh: a pedagogical approach
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mohd. Mustafa Kamal

Ladakh's unique landscape, climate, landforms, and cultural heritage make it an unparalleled destination to learn geography. This article lists effective pedagogical strategies employed by teachers in this region to contextualize geography and make it more comprehensible and engaging for children.

NEP 2020 and NCF-SE 2023

National Education Policy 2020 and National Curriculum Framework-School Education 2023 promote conceptual learning as opposed to rote learning. Ladakh, being a relatively new union territory, does not have a State Council of Educational Research and Training (SCERT) as yet. As such, the school education department cannot tailor the curriculum to local requirements and develop textbooks that are context-specific. Ladakh is a model, in this sense, to adopt the recommendations made by NEP 2020 and NCF-SE 2023, particularly in social science education.

Understanding mountains and landforms

Educators here help students understand the dramatic mountain landscapes of Ladakh, including the Zanskar and Ladakh ranges, through fieldtrips and practical lessons. There are different mountain ranges, high and low peaks, narrow valleys, gorges, and deep reliefs all around. These landscapes provide a natural learning environment to understand and analyze soil profiles, types of rocks, process of rock formations, etc. The study of mountains and their environmental impacts become tangible and memorable this way. In addition, observation of the sediments, different types of rocks and soils add value to the learning.

Rivers and their role

Teachers here emphasize the contributions of rivers such as the Suru, Zanskar, and Shyok tributaries of the Indus River, to daily life in Ladakh. Students are shown around different nallas (small rivers) and confluence sites to enable learning about tributaries and the development of the main river system. Lab activities such as water quality determination and the khul irrigation system study enable experiential learning.

Addressing climate change and glaciers

Climate and the effects of climate change are vital topics in geography education. In an effort to contribute to environment conservation, teachers familiarize students with concepts like artificial glaciers (ice stupas), which are a common phenomena in every Ladakhi village to deal with the growing water scarcity and to help recharge ground water. They also discuss the critical issue of receding glaciers like Nun Kun Glacier, Parkachik Glacier, etc. The subject covers the effect of these changes on indigenous people as well as their options for adaptation, inviting learners to know more about the environment.

The Middle School Karith Ice Stupa Initiative is a study in experiential learning that can be applied to inspire environmental consciousness and practices of resilience at the community level. The project was led by school Head Teacher Mohammad Ali, National Teacher Awardee, in December 2016. It involves teachers and school students building ice stupas. Apart from acquiring relevant knowledge of pertinent topics such as water management and global warming, they also indirectly contribute to enhancing the resilience of their community against such challenges. The ice stupa stores winter water in the form of ice and releases it during the spring planting season when the stupa starts melting. This practical approach not only educates students about sustainable practices but also benefits the local community. The ice stupa project has won many awards, including National Water Award 2018, Government of India.

Cloudbursts and flash floods in Leh and Kargil

The frequency of cloudbursts and flash floods in Ladakh has increased many folds and the climate has become unpredictable. Leh witnessed huge flash floods, the first of their kind on August 6, 2010 and Kargil in 2006, 2010, 2013 and so on. These phenomena have provided teachers with real-life examples to help students understand the impact of climate change to the fragile ecosystem of Ladakh. These catastrophes have caused extensive damage to homes, agricultural lands, and infrastructure. In order to provide firsthand experience and to explain the causes and impacts of climate change, teachers accompany their students to these areas wherever possible.

Crop patterns and traditional foods

Educators discuss subsistence agriculture by highlighting crop patterns, including wheat and barley, some pulses, fodder, etc. Students learn about traditional farming techniques and the importance of these crops in Ladakhi diets. They are also given adequate knowledge about the distribution of crop patterns and climate conditions in other states of India. This helps understand why only wheat, barley, and crops that need less water and moderate temperature are cultivable here. Exploring traditional foods like thukpa and paba adds a cultural dimension to geography lessons.

Pastoral nomads and the Changpa tribe

The pastoral nomads of Ladakh, particularly the Changpa tribe, provide insights into nomadic lifestyles. Teachers explain the Changpa's livelihood, migration patterns, and challenges posed by climate change. Visits to nomadic settlements offer students valuable real-life experiences.

Studying soil profiles, landforms, and erosion

Understanding soil profiles and erosion in Ladakh's diverse landscapes is crucial for students. Teachers cover soil composition, formation processes, and the effects of erosion on agriculture and settlements by taking students to soil profile areas, small plateaus like Kurbathang Plateau in Kargil, steep slopes, and plains like Taru Thang in Leh. Fieldwork and soil testing activities reinforce these lessons, making them more relatable. Observing soil erosion is again a matter of routine natural phenomena for the students, as often in the evenings, many parts of Ladakh witness short windy spells.

Transportation and mountain passes

Mountain passes play a very crucial role in helping the people of Ladakh access basic facilities. So, knowledge of the mountain passes and their connections in the life of Ladakhis become paramount for students in Ladakh. Teachers discuss the significance of strategic mountain passes, Zojila, Penzila, Fotula, Khardungla, and Rotangla, which connect Kargil with Srinagar (Kashmir); Kargil with Zanskar; Leh with Nubra and Manali respectively. Lessons cover the historical and current importance of these passes, maintenance challenges, and their role in connecting Ladakh to other regions.

Understanding the international border

The historical Silk Route connects Leh with Yarkhend and Kargil with Skardu. Furthermore, the Line of Actual Control (LAC) between India and China in Leh district and Line of Control (LOC) between India and Pakistan in Kargil are also integrated into lessons on border systems and geopolitics. Their accessibility, albeit limited, allows students to experience firsthand the unique lifestyle* of the border areas integrated with challenges and opportunities.

Historical sculptures and cultural heritage

The big rock cut statues of Maitreya and Avaloketeshwara in Karchay, Mulbekh, Tumail, and Drass showcase the cultural history of Ladakh. Petroglyphs along the Kargil-Zankar National Highway, particularly near Khilmarchy Sankoo, and the Kanika stupa in Sani Zanskar are also part of this legacy. Apart from that, the region has Middle Eastern-style buildings, particularly in Kargil district, such as Imam Bargahs, khanqahs (hostels), and sacred mosques. Students can learn how these structures have contributed to the aesthetics, architecture, history, and culture of Ladakh.

Cave museum initiative at Government Model Middle School, Pashkum, Kargil

I spearhead the cave museum initiative, where students act as junior archaeologists. They explore cave shelters that were dug during the 1999 Kargil War by the people of Pashkum village in Kargil district to take shelter from the firing and also the ruins of forts built by local chieftains during the medieval period in Ladakh. This way, they get to learn about the region's history and cultural heritage. This project instills questioning and curiosity in students, making lessons in social science more interesting, deeply connected, and effective. In this initiative, students are provided with hands-on experience of painting the walls of caves. Additionally, the replica making activity of seals, coins, toys, etc., from fine clay is another routine exercise of the initiative.

Conclusion

Teaching geography in Ladakh involves dynamic methods that make the subject engaging and comprehensible for school students. Through practical activities, field trips, and cultural interactions, educators create an immersive learning experience that fosters geographical knowledge and respect for Ladakh's unique environment and heritage. Moreover, in the absence of an SCERT, this approach acts as a bridge to contextualize concepts, processes, and events in the textbooks of social science, especially geography education.

*Villagers mainly rely on potter jobs at military posts on the border. They raise ponies because the Indian army pays well for using them to transport essential supplies, like food and ammunition, to these posts. Moreover, the army contributes in developing basic infrastructure like health and education. At times, the army also takes students on exposure tours (Bharat Darshan).

Mohd. Mustafa Kamal is a Ladakh state awardee and teacher, School Education Department, Kargil, Ladakh. He can be reached at mustakmal56817@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mohd. Mustafa Kamal
From glacier walks to soil testing, teachers in Ladakh are redefining geography education. Mohd. Mustafa Kamal shares how students get to explore ice stupas, mountain passes, and cave shelters.

item.title: Innovative pedagogy and assessment in geography
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Manjula Raman

Geography has been one of my favourite subjects since school because of my teacher Miss Violet who would ask us to tuck our tummies in, sit straight, close our textbooks, and enjoy the class, which was all about adventure and exploration.

We were able to travel the globe, learn different cultures, and know how people live on the planet without stepping out of our seats. Maps, puzzles, and quizzes made the lessons fun and interactive. At a time when there was no multimedia, a good map-hunt or trivia games used to be so much fun. Miss Violet would talk of volcanoes, earthquakes, deserts, oceans, and other wonderful phenomena of the world. We always felt a real-world connection between geography, history, and mathematics. Miss Violet transformed her students into geographers by inculcating curiosity, promoting exploration, and making learning interactive.

My experience as a student made it very clear to me that geography teaching-learning has to be hands-on and interactive, only then will students be really engaged. Geography is not just about maps, it is about real-world problems like urban planning, climate change, disaster management, transportation, etc., and a teacher should design her lessons to bring this across to her students. In this article, I share how we can make geography lessons fun and the different ways to assess student learning in the subject.

Making geography teaching interesting

Here are some ideas to inject some fun into your lessons.

  • Use digital maps and Google Earth to let students explore the world.
  • Play geography-based games like Geoguessr or treasure hunt using coordinates.
  • Compare old and new photographs to see how a community has caused the environment to change over the years.
  • Bring in music, literature, folklore, cuisine, and clothes from different regions of the world.
  • Ask students to study the label of a product and identify where it originated from and research about the place.
  • Students can pick two cities and study how they are connected and linked by human migration patterns, economics, concerns, mineral deposits, communication systems, and transport.
  • Travelling is the best geography lesson, hence visits to natural parks, museums, or even your neighbourhood will make for a good lesson in geography.
  • Project-based learning, researching a country, preparing travel brochures, play-acting as a weather reporter, or simply putting together a travel journal with pictures of landscapes, cities, landforms are all interesting activities.
  • Google Earth can also be used to help learners go on virtual fieldtrips to distant and well-known places. Watching shows such as Planet Earth or those on National Geographic channel is a fascinating means of acquiring information.
  • Adapt lessons to various learning types. For instance, for a visual learner, infographics and videos can be extremely engaging. For a kinesthetic learner, constructing 3D models of landscape may generate a lot of interest, and an auditory learner perhaps will enjoy listening to songs from various countries, learning languages, and listening to debates on international issues.
  • Create a geography center, which includes a variety of maps, atlases, globes, books, pictures, artifacts, etc.

Assessment techniques

Assessment is an essential tool for teachers to measure student learning and ensure real-world application of knowledge. Innovative assessment tools will combine summative and formative assessments and guarantee a balanced evaluation.

Conventional assessment instruments include multiple-choice quizzes, tests, short answers and essay questions, and map work and interpretation.

Let us now look at some innovative ways in which to assess students.

  • Graphic organizers, flowcharts, and Venn diagrams are interesting assessment devices as they allow students to visually compare and contrast concepts, ideas or objects, aiding in the organization of thought and identification of similarities and differences.
  • Organized exercises for concept application can be an approach to assess deep learning of geography.
  • Performance-based assessment tools can be used to evaluate and measure skills and application in geography. Case studies, for instance, can be used to assess student reasoning, problem-solving and decision-making skills.
  • Geography enquiry projects, whereby students conduct research and investigate topics such as geomorphology, urban planning, pollution mitigation, etc.
  • Technology based assessment tools which are interactive and digital, such as GIS mapping, Google Earth for special analysis, and map based interactive games.
  • Project-based and collaborative assessment instruments include students constructing physical or virtual models of landforms, cities, or entire ecosystems. Creating travel brochures, reflective journals, and involving students in sustainable development and community based projects all help assess geography learning.

Teachers are essential in turning geography into a relevant and interesting subject using creative pedagogy and evaluation tools. With technology, practical applications, and hands-on learning, they can provide rich learning experiences that transcend mere memorization. Effective teaching should be complemented by good assessment practices that not only test knowledge, but also problem-solving and critical thinking skills. Through continuous refinement of teaching and assessment practices, teachers can promote geographical literacy, curiosity, and enhanced understanding of global issues. By embracing innovation, teachers inspire learners to be active thinkers and responsible global citizens, capable of navigating and shaping their world.

Manjula Raman is a life-long learner and a kaizen practitioner focusing on making small incremental improvements everyday. She calls herself a globe trotter, having visited 70% of the world, thanks to her geography teacher who sowed compelling images of the world by her extra ordinary geography lessons. She is committed to the students under her care and wishes to see them as thought-leaders. She can be reached at manjularaman@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Manjula Raman
How can assessments be as engaging as field trips? Dr. Manjula Raman shares how creative evaluations can transform geography education.

item.title: Learning geography through projects
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sharmila Govande

A class of grade 5 students, equipped with their charts, models, and demonstrations, wait eagerly for their geography teacher. They have worked on their projects in pairs for the past six weeks. Each pair had to select a topic aligned to what was taught in their geography syllabus. They then had to conduct a thorough research using both secondary and primary sources and put together the information they had acquired in a creative way and present it to the class.

Aryan and Vedh had worked on the topic of animal shelters. They had explored shelters of various animals with a focus on shelters in different climatic conditions. They had researched burrows, caves, lodges, ant colonies, nests, and dens. They looked at how climatic conditions played a role in the materials used to build shelters and how the shape of the shelter differed with the region. They had worked with their hands to create a model of a lodge – a beaver’s home. They spoke with excitement how the beaver’s home withstood rapids, harsh weather, and predators.

Their project – which was completed within the regular geography classes – is a classic example of what “Project Based Learning” (PBL) is. PBL is a dynamic approach, where students actively explore real-world situations, problems, and challenges while acquiring deeper knowledge. When applied to geography, PBL can help young learners connect with their surroundings, understand global issues, and develop essential skills such as research, collaboration, and creativity.

Project based learning and geography

Geography is more than just learning about maps and places; it encompasses the study of Earth's landscapes, environments, and how humans interact with them. It helps children develop a sense of place and understand cultural diversity, environmental sustainability, and global interconnectivity.

Geography can be a bridge between science and social studies, linking topics such as climate change, natural disasters, migration, and urban planning. By using PBL in geography, students are encouraged to explore these topics through hands-on, meaningful experiences.

Enhances engagement and motivation

PBL shifts the focus from passive learning to active participation. Students take ownership of their learning, making geography more exciting and relevant. For Aryan and Vedh, their excitement was unbound. Another pair had taken “Iceland” as their topic. They explored the climate, topography, crops and vegetation, and clothing and culture of the people living in Iceland. The motivation among the students was high, their minds were alert, and their queries were relevant. They were all immersed in their work unlike students who usually doze off in a geography class.

Develops critical thinking and problem-solving skills

Projects require students to analyze real-world issues, ask questions, and develop solutions, fostering deeper understanding and higher-order thinking. A few years ago, I was teaching a class of 8 to 10 year olds. The first project assigned to them was to create a map of their classroom, followed by a map of their floor, and a map of the school premise. The following year, I asked them to work on a map of their surroundings. As they engaged in their tasks, I saw their minds working out measurements, proportions, and spatial organization. These young students learned to use a scale, protractor, and other geometry equipment too. I could see integration of geography and geometry along with critical thinking and problem-solving skills.

Encourages collaboration and communication

PBL can involve individual projects, paired work, or group work. One can witness brainstorming, exchange of ideas and notes, cooperation, collaboration along with disagreements and arguments. When students are given time within class hours to work on their projects, the classroom becomes a fertile ground for learning life skills.

Connects learning to real-world issues

Students explore real-world topics like climate change, urbanization, and cultural diversity, making learning more meaningful and applicable to their lives. Arya and Vedh who were studying animal shelters were curious about what happens to these natural shelters and the animals with rapid urbanization and receding forest cover. They expressed concern over rivers drying up and animals losing their habitats.

Integrates technology and research skills

Using digital tools, mapping software, and online resources enhances students’ research abilities, preparing them for the digital age. Such projects help students explore the usage of internet sources for gaining more knowledge and thereby rethink the use of mobile phones, laptops, and tablets beyond playing games or scrolling through mindless content.

Integrates other subjects

PBL has the potential to integrate various subjects such as languages, math, science, geography, and history within a project making knowledge acquisition more holistic and relevant. Aryan and Ved integrated science, math, and geography within their project of animal shelters. Their enquiry also led them to question civic issues of rapid urbanization.

Implementing PBL in geography

I have witnessed that Project Based Learning has been reduced to allocation of the projects to students who are asked to complete them at home and submit them on the date decided by the school. From a beautiful learning experience, they have become a burden that is conveniently transferred to the parents.

For PBL to be successful, schools need to allot time and resources within school hours and the teacher to become a mentor and guide during the process. The teacher needs to take on the responsibility to keep the excitement and the quest for learning and doing among the students. Keeping in mind that every child is unique and their learnings are unique, teachers need to be open about the learning insights students have while working on projects. A few pointers for teachers:

  1. Allocate time within school hours for project work. Don’t expect students to complete the projects at home. Project based learning integrated within school hours will reap maximum impact.
  2. The role of the teacher is to facilitate learning. In PBL, teachers become facilitators. Their role changes from teaching something to enabling students to research, think, reason, and make sense of their chosen topic and explore creative ways to display their learning.
  3. Encourage inquiry-based learning, i.e., ask questions and find answers. The teacher's role is to avoid giving answers and facilitate a process where the students look for the answers through research, experiments, talking to experts, etc.
  4. Provide access to resources – both online and offline. The teacher’s role is to support the students by providing access to information, tools, and equipment required for the project.
  5. Promote collaboration among students to exchange information, help each other in their projects.
  6. Assess through reflection and presentation. Focus on quality of research, reflection, and their presentation.

Conclusion

Project Based Learning in geography provides students with an immersive, engaging, and meaningful way to explore the world around them. Through hands-on projects, students develop essential academic and life skills, fostering curiosity and a deeper understanding of global issues. By implementing PBL strategies, educators can create a classroom environment where geography is not just a subject but an adventure in learning.

Sharmila Govande is an unschooling mom, parenting guide, and a social development professional. She can be reached at sharmilagovande@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sharmila Govande
Project-Based Learning (PBL) can transform geography classes from one-way lectures to more interesting and engaging sessions. Sharmila Govande shares how it could even help connect students to real-world issues like climate change.

item.title: Harnessing the augmented and the digital: rethinking space and geography through games
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aditya Deshbandhu

I write this article in my capacity as a researcher who engages with games and playful experiences in academic environments. When you consider games as digital artifacts and experiences, few things are more important than understanding how they utilize space. Whether it is the way players are expected to engage with it (think in-game maps, landmarks, range of effect), manipulate it to overcome challenges (puzzles, character abilities), or internalize specific elements depending on the nature of game worlds that digital games often offer – understanding how space can operate in virtual environments is a prerequisite for enabling the magic of games.

However, no matter how magical a game is, the space it offers players is often a representation of how we perceive it in our offline contexts. For example, most digital games follow the same rules of gravity as seen around us. Similarly, aspects of weather, nature, and vegetation are also derived from the physical world. For example, on spending time in a game like Forza Horizon 5 (2021) or The Crew Motorfest (2022) you are bound to observe that the recreations of Mexico in the former and the scaled-down versions of the Hawaiian Islands O’ahu and Maui in the latter are not just CGI (Computer Generated Imagery) models based on satellite imagery and maps, but are actually digital sites with the same biomes, ecosystems, and weather patterns.

Digital environments and experiences have become increasingly adept at capturing information and data from our surroundings to render interactive spaces that are not just immersive but also spark curiosity, encourage exploration, and reward discovery. In the process, players through their repetitive acts of play, find new ways to transform these spaces into sites of learning. I believe it is essential for us to harness these playful sites and find ways to connect them to the ideas and discussions from our classrooms. For example, in my time with Forza Horizon 5, I was able to drive my cars through swamps, marshes, forests, waterfalls, deserts, and mountains. In one of the game’s missions, I was even required to go atop a volcano to collect samples of volcanic ash from the crater and witness the volcanic activity. Towards the end of the mission, in true dramatic fashion, the game got me to race away from the volcano just as the lava erupted around me. Most people will think about how this is a cool concept for a game, but I would like to highlight that this particular sequence would be ideal for a classroom where students are being introduced to volcanoes. Where in the world could we offer a safe and controlled field trip to an active volcano except in a virtual environment rendered by such a game? If you feel that just seeing a volcano erupt up-close isn’t enough, then let me tell you that with a controller that offers haptic and vibrational feedback, players can also feel the waxing rhythms of the ground rumbling as the volcanic activity picks up.

If volcanic activity and spewing lava isn’t enough to make my case then let me offer a few more examples where digital games and virtual environments can be utilized in today’s geography classrooms. From augmented reality services like Pokémon Go and Ingress to our understanding of spatiality in virtual reality, in this article I offer four examples to consider how game-based learning can be integrated into the curriculum.

  1. Pokémon Goand Ingress

The first two games on this list are some of the most popular augmented apps available. Pokémon Go is particularly popular because of the franchise it draws from and the unique form of nostalgia it offers players by letting them enjoy their own journeys as trainers of the iconic creatures[*]. Academic research about the game has indicated how engagement with it not only gets players to physically move and get out of their comfort zones to progress in-game, but also perform purpose-driven acts of leisure. In my own research with the game’s players, I have observed them join and create networks, organize events around the game, and collectively make decisions about travelling to specific locations. Most of these acts highlight players’ willingness to show initiative and actively create meaningful connections both with fellow players as well as with the game’s architecture and mechanics.

In my classes whenever I have used either of the two games (Pokémon Go or Ingress), I have found students spending a considerable amount of time getting acquainted with the landmarks and physical locations that the game deems important enough to serve as sites for in-game activity. For example, what locations are deemed worthy of being Pokéstops or Gyms or spaces to find specific kinds of Pokémon? This curiosity often leads them to engage with the history of these sites and the geographic markers that characterize them. In the Indian classroom of today, where one can’t guarantee spaces for play like grounds and fields (especially in urban settings), augmented games allow players to carve their own journeys of play and learning in the hybrid sites they offer. I have witnessed friends and fellow-players come to terms with new cities and towns through Pokémon Go or Ingress. The challenge, then, is can we tap this avenue for everyday learning in the classroom? Assessments for long weekends or mid-year vacation breaks could be tailor-made around augmented reality journeys of students and this could be a great way to start maybe?

2. Flight Simulator

The second game on this list is what I would call a live-game experience – a game where real-time travel and weather data are amalgamated by the game engine to make play possible. Historically, Flight Simulator has been a great game to understand aviation, aeronautics, and even basic physics, but in its current live game avatar it becomes a valuable site for students of geography and international affairs as it combines satellite imagery, data from Microsoft’s maps, travel routes, weather data, and street views to render cities and their landmarks in almost accurate real-time fashion. Someone creative could use the game and its weather engine to show students cloud patterns, types of precipitation, phenomena like the monsoon, occurrences like cyclones, and even a variety of global landmarks. In many ways a game engine like Microsoft’s Flight Simulator is an interactive 3D-atlas at your disposal and everything from the Coliseum in Rome to the Taj Mahal and the Sydney Opera House are at your fingertips to be seen up close from various kinds of aerial views. The onus then lies on us as designers of curricula to imagine how we integrate the capabilities of a platform like this into our classrooms to facilitate discussions rooted in experiential learning.

3. Beat Saber/Beat Labs

The third game on this list is a virtual reality experience and is more difficult to integrate into standard geographical curricula, but it offers one of the best understandings of space and boundaries from a personal standpoint. It gets users of the virtual reality (VR) headset to visualize the dimensions and boundaries of their bodies and view virtual and blended realities through the pass-through that the headsets offer. While geography curricula in middle and high schools in India don’t introduce students to the idea of human geography and think about the Anthropocene, at the undergraduate and post graduate level, critically thinking about how we as a race interact with the environment and the ramifications of our cultural, social, and economic practices is an essential requisite. A virtual reality experience like Beat Saber and Beat Labs is inherently based on music, but is a great way to internalize our corporeal presence and allows us to view our activities and habits from an objective position.

Other virtual reality experiences similar to Beat Saber allow users to experience underwater travel, or the vastness of space – engagements that can be both informative and transformative for young students.

4. Virtual and Mixed Reality Tours

The last set of ideas in this article continues to engage with the augmented but focuses on aspects of mixed reality (MR), which don’t need a dedicated VR/MR headset. On the internet and even on the Windows operating system, you could find access to virtual and mixed reality tours of unique historical sites that can be part of the classroom. For example, a free virtual tour of historical Machu Picchu (Machu Picchu 360) is available online, courtesy its makers Cypha and 360 Fly, which is both informative and interactive. In the classroom setting, one could use it in a history class to introduce archaeology, the Inca civilization, but in a geography class it would be ideal to showcase the Andes as a mountain range and also to offer a primer on the prevalent ecosystem. Similar tours are available for a variety of sites and also some of the most popular museums including the Smithsonian. The potential to experiment with MR and VR tours is significant provided you find the requisite support in terms of infrastructure and connectivity – aspects that are always equally challenging and elusive.

As we consider these four ideas and make room for several others, it is also necessary for us to reframe geography as a discipline/subject in school curricula. Often considered a subject that must be endured as part of the curriculum, and one that doesn’t seem to offer much to inform students’ careers – geography isn’t flashy or exciting enough for most students or their parents. However, as evidenced by all the examples above, it is abundantly clear that from mapping and building augmented experiences to designing immersive environments, a foundational understanding of geography and its concepts is a must for the digital worker of the 21st century. Thus, it is essential for us as facilitators of learning to ensure that our students understand this lest they find that in striving to excel at the technical they have completely overlooked the human.

[*]Most players of the game have spoken about playing with iconic Pokémon like Pikachu, Bulbasaur, Charmander, and other legendary Pokémon like Mewtwo, Rayquaza, and Groudon as primary motivation for their first engagement with the game.

Aditya Deshbandhu is a Senior Lecturer of communications, digital media sociology at the University of Exeter, UK. He is the author of the books Gaming Culture(s) in India: Digital Play in Everyday Life and the just released, The 21st Century in a Hundred games. He can be reached at a.deshbandhu@exeter.ac.uk.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aditya Deshbandhu
The virtual and augmented environments of digital games can make geography more engaging for today’s learners. Aditya Deshbandhu shares some examples.

item.title: Seeing, sensing, sustaining: bringing India’s ecosystems to life
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Somnath Mukherjee

Teaching geography in today's fast-evolving educational world is a bit like being a storyteller and a scientist rolled into one. We're not just handing out facts, we're helping students truly understand how geography shapes everything from tiny ecosystems to massive climate patterns and how it all connects to human life.

But let's be honest, getting students excited about these big, interconnected ideas can be tough. I’ll never forget the day when a student looked at me, puzzled, and asked, “Sir, why are we even studying ecosystems in geography? Isn’t that a biology thing?” That question hit me hard. It made me realize how often we accidentally divide learning into separate boxes, when in reality everything's linked. Ecosystems aren't just about plants and animals; they're deeply rooted in geography.

Think about it. The amazing mangrove forests of the Sundarbans exist because of the way the Ganges and Brahmaputra rivers formed that delta. The lush Western Ghats are green because of the way the mountains force air upwards, creating rainfall. Even the harsh Thar Desert is shaped by global wind patterns and the way mountains block rain.

So, how do we make all this real for our students? How do we bring these huge, complex systems into our classrooms and make them feel relevant? How do we help them feel the urgency of deforestation in the Western Ghats or the impact of melting glaciers in the Himalayas?

This is where teaching aids can be of use – these are tools that can turn abstract ideas into something students can actually see, touch, and explore.

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and the National Curriculum Framework (NCF) 2023 are pushing us to create learning that's hands-on (experiential), connects different subjects (interdisciplinary), and lets students ask questions (inquiry-based). Geography, with its natural focus on how humans and the environment interact, is perfect for this!

Then, why is teaching ‘ecosystems’ in geography sometimes a battle?

That's because unlike dates in history or formulas in math, ecosystems are constantly changing, shaped by both nature and humans. You can't just show a map and expect students to "get it". And many geographical processes happen over very different time scales – a river eroding a bank is fast, but the formation of a mountain range takes millions of years!

In my classroom, I've seen students struggle most with seeing the connections between things. For example, when we first talked about the Western Ghats, they just saw them as lines on a map. It wasn't until we used Google Earth to fly over them and see the elevation changes that the students understood how those mountains create the rain that feeds the forests and shapes the incredible variety of life there.

Seeing "before-and-after" images of the Sundarbans also drove home the reality of rising sea levels and deforestation in a way that words alone couldn't.

So, what makes a teaching aid truly good?

A good teaching aid is like a key that unlocks understanding. It takes a concept that's floating in the air and makes it solid, interactive, and meaningful. But how do we choose the right tool for the right topic?

To ensure that a teaching aid meets the needs of both students and curriculum goals, I follow the SMART approach:

By using the SMART checklist, we can make sure our teaching aids are really helping students develop their "geographical thinking" – their ability to understand spatial relationships, think critically about the world, and connect what they learn to real-life situations.

Let's get practical! How do we use these ideas to create exciting, hands-on learning about ecosystems?

Diving into the Sundarbans using Google Earth

Google Earth is like a magic carpet for geography teachers. It lets you and your students explore the world with super-detailed satellite images and 3D models. It's not just a fancy map, it's a way to see patterns and changes over time.

The Sundarbans, the world's largest mangrove forest, is perfect for a Google Earth adventure. One of the most impactful lessons I conducted was a Google Earth exploration of the Sundarbans. I divided students into groups, each focusing on a specific aspect of the ecosystem – mangrove distribution, tidal networks, human impact, and climate change. As students zoomed in on satellite images, they were surprised to see the shrinking mangrove cover. One student, after noticing the extent of coastal erosion asked, “Sir, does this mean one day Sundarbans will disappear?” This led to a discussion on climate resilience and conservation efforts.

Activity: Sundarbans analysis using Google Earth

This activity helps students explore the Sundarbans, identify key geographical features, and think critically about how humans and the environment interact.

Materials
• Computers with internet access
• Google Earth (web or desktop version)
• Satellite imagery of the Sundarbans
• Worksheets with guiding questions

Procedure

  1. Exploration (20 minutes)
    • Students open Google Earth and search for Sundarbans, India/Bangladesh.
    • Identify and label key features such as mangrove forests, tidal channels, human settlements, and protected areas.
    • Compare current and past satellite images to analyze environmental changes over time.
  2. Analysis and discussion (15 minutes)
    • How has human activity impacted the Sundarbans?
    • What role do mangroves play in coastal protection?
    • How can remote sensing contribute to conservation efforts?
  3. Conclusion and reflection (10 minutes)
    • Students summarize their findings and discuss the importance of satellite imagery in environmental monitoring.
    • Encourage students to propose solutions for sustainable ecosystem management.
  4. Geographic concepts: Spatial distribution, delta formation, coastal geomorphology, remote sensing, human-environment interaction.
  5. Assessment: Students participation in discussions, accuracy of labels, thoroughness of analysis, and proposed solutions.
  6. Differentiation
    • For students who need more support, provide a list of key features to find.
    • For advanced students, challenge them to research specific conservation projects or analyze changes in salinity levels.

Visualizing ecosystems through hand-drawn posters and digital infographics

Posters and infographics are powerful visual tools that simplify complex ecological concepts. By creating hand-drawn posters or digital infographics, students engage in spatial thinking and critical analysis, allowing them to communicate environmental challenges effectively. This method helps students connect classroom learning to real-world conservation issues.

In one classroom activity, I asked students to design posters illustrating the impact of human activities on Indian ecosystems. One group, working on the Thar Desert, created a striking “Desertification Then vs. Now” poster, showing how land degradation had intensified over time. Another group focused on the Western Ghats, depicting how deforestation has led to landslides. These visual projects helped students engage with real-world environmental issues in a meaningful way.

Activity: Poster creation on ecosystems

Students will analyze and create posters to understand conservation efforts and human impact on ecosystems.

Materials

  • Posters by students on ecosystems (hand-drawn or digital)
  • Chart paper, colours, markers, and other art supplies
  • ICT tools for digital infographics (Canva, PowerPoint, etc.)

Procedure

  1. Poster creation (30 minutes)
    • Students select an ecosystem they are studying and design a poster or infographic.
    • They illustrate conservation challenges and possible solutions using visual storytelling.
  2. Introduction (10 minutes)
    • Display the student-created posters on different ecosystems.
    • Discuss the role of visual representation in geography education.
  3. Analysis and discussion (15 minutes)
    • Students examine posters and discuss elements like biodiversity, conservation strategies, and environmental threats.
    • Encourage students to identify human impacts and propose sustainable solutions.
  4. Presentation and class discussion (15 minutes)
    • Each student presents their poster and explains the ecological issues represented.
    • Classmates provide feedback and discuss different conservation strategies.
  5. Reflection (10 minutes)
    • How did creating a poster help in understanding the ecosystem?
    • How does visual learning enhance the retention of ecological concepts? Through this activity, students don’t just learn about ecosystems – they interpret, analyze, and creatively express their understanding, making geography learning immersive and meaningful.
  6. Geographic concepts: Environmental perception, human-environment interaction, visual representation of geographic data, conservation geography.
  7. Assessment
    • Clarity of environmental messages in the posters.
    • Creativity of visual communication.
    • Use of evidence to explain conservation challenges.
    • Class participation.
  8. Differentiation
    • For students who struggle, provide pre-drawn templates or simplified storylines.
    • For advanced learners, challenge them to research more complex ecological interactions or create digital animations.

The Thar desert through National Geographic’s MapMaker
National Geographic’s MapMaker enhances geographic learning by providing interactive mapping tools that allow students to create and analyze thematic maps, develop their ability to interpret geographic data, and draw connections between physical landscapes and biodiversity. The tool promotes active learning and critical thinking by enabling students to investigate real-world geographic phenomena.

Activity: Exploring desert ecosystems using National Geographic’s MapMaker
Students will use National Geographic’s MapMaker to explore the Thar desert, analyze its topography, and assess environmental challenges.

Materials
• Computers with internet access
• National Geographic’s MapMaker (online platform)
• Worksheets with analysis questions

Procedure

  1. Introduction (10 minutes)
    • Brief discussion on desert ecosystems and their characteristics.
    • Introduction to the Thar desert as a case study.
  2. Exploration (20 minutes)
    • Students access National Geographic MAPMAKER and locate the Thar desert.
    • Use elevation profiles to study how topography affects vegetation and climate.
    • Overlay climate and population density layers to understand human-environment interactions.
  3. Analysis and discussion (15 minutes)
    • How does topography influence temperature and precipitation in the Thar desert?
    • What major environmental challenges does this ecosystem face?
  4. Conclusion and reflection (10 minutes)
    • Encourage students to propose conservation strategies based on their map interpretations.
  5. Geographic concepts: Cartography, spatial analysis, thematic mapping, climate geography, human-environment interaction.
  6. Assessment
    • Accuracy of map interpretations.
    • Ability to analyze spatial relationships.
    • Critical thinking about environmental challenges.
    • Participation in discussions.
  7. Differentiation
    • Provide students with pre-selected map layers or guided questions to focus their exploration.
    • Challenge advanced students to create their own thematic maps using additional datasets.

The Himalayas through bulletin boards and models
Bulletin boards and models encourage interactive and student-driven learning. They allow students to engage in hands-on activities that enhance their spatial thinking, teamwork, and critical analysis skills. Bulletin boards provide a continuous visual reference that reinforces key concepts, while models offer tangible representations of ecosystems, making abstract ideas more concrete.

Activity: Creating a bulletin board on The Himalayan ecosystem
Students will research and design a bulletin board to illustrate the key components of the Himalayan ecosystem in Jammu and Kashmir, fostering engagement and collaborative learning.

Materials
• Chart papers, markers, printed images
• ICT tools for research
• Coloured pins and display boards
• Maps of Jammu and Kashmir

Procedure

  1. Introduction (10 minutes)
    • Explain how bulletin boards can effectively communicate geographic and ecological concepts.
    • Provide a brief overview of the Himalayan ecosystem in Jammu and Kashmir.
  2. Research and design (30 minutes)
    • Divide students into groups and assign each group a specific aspect of the Himalayan ecosystem (e.g., biodiversity, climate, human impact, conservation efforts).
    • Students research their topics using ICT tools and compile key facts, images, maps, and statistics.
    • Groups design the bulletin board with labelled sections highlighting their findings.
  3. Presentation (15 minutes)
    • Each group presents their section of the bulletin board to the class, explaining their research and key takeaways.
    • Classmates ask questions and provide feedback, facilitating discussion and knowledge sharing.
  4. Discussion and reflection (10 minutes)
    • How did creating the bulletin board improve understanding of the Himalayan ecosystem?
    • What challenges did students face in synthesizing and presenting geographic data?
    • How does the Himalayan ecosystem in Jammu and Kashmir compare with other ecosystems the students have studied?
  5. Geographic concepts: Biogeography, altitudinal zonation, climate geography, human-environment interaction, resource management.
  6. Assessment
    • Accuracy of information presented on the bulletin board.
    • Clarity of visual representation.
    • Teamwork and collaboration.
    • Presentation skills and class participation.
  7. Differentiation
    • Provide students with pre-selected research topics or templates for the bulletin board layout.
    • Challenge advanced students to incorporate interactive elements or multimedia into their presentations.

Understanding wetland ecosystems through comic strips
Comic strips provide a structured yet creative way to communicate ecological knowledge. They break down complex environmental interactions into smaller, digestible parts, making learning more engaging. By combining visual elements with narrative techniques, comic strips stimulate interest in geographic topics while reinforcing scientific concepts. Additionally, they cater to diverse learning styles, helping students who may struggle with traditional text-heavy materials grasp key ideas effectively.

Activity: Students will use comic strips to illustrate the importance of wetland ecosystems and the need for conservation efforts.

Materials
• Teacher-created comic strip (using Canva) on the importance of lakes
• Blank sheets, colour pencils, markers
• ICT tools for digital comic creation

Procedure

  1. Introduction (10 minutes)
    • The teacher presents a comic strip explaining the role of lakes in human life.
    • Discussion on how storytelling can simplify ecological concepts.
  2. Creative task (30 minutes)
    • Students individually create a comic strip on the uniqueness of a specific Indian lake, focusing on its ecosystem, biodiversity, and conservation challenges.
  3. Presentation and discussion (15 minutes)
    • Students showcase their comic strips and explain their narratives.
    • Class discussion on the role of wetlands in sustaining ecosystems.
  4. Geographic concepts: Hydrology, biogeography, human-environment interaction, conservation geography, environmental storytelling.
  5. Assessment
    • Clarity and accuracy of information presented in the comic strip.
    • Creativity and effectiveness of the narrative.
    • Use of visual elements to enhance understanding.
    • Understanding of conservation challenges.
  6. Differentiation
    • Provide students with pre-written scripts or storyboard templates.
    • Encourage students to focus on specific aspects of the wetland ecosystem based on their interests.

Learning about the ecosystem of the Western Ghats through gamification
Gamification enhances student engagement by making learning dynamic and participatory. Board games and digital simulations encourage students to apply ecological knowledge in decision-making scenarios, reinforcing critical thinking and problem-solving skills. Games also introduce an element of competition and collaboration, which fosters teamwork and deeper engagement with geographic concepts.

Activity: The Western Ghats ecosystem board game
Students will engage in a board game designed to reinforce knowledge of the Western Ghats ecosystem, including biodiversity, climate, and conservation challenges.

Materials
• Custom board game with 21 ecosystem-related questions
• Dice
• Erasers (used as game pieces)

Procedure

  1. Introduction (10 minutes)
    • Provide an overview of the Western Ghats ecosystem, including key features, species, and environmental threats.
  2. Gameplay (30 minutes)
    • Each student rolls the dice; the highest number starts the game.
    • Players roll the dice on their turn and move their eraser game piece forward.
    • When landing on a question space, the student must answer an ecosystem-related question.
    • Correct answers allow students to stay on their spot, while incorrect answers require them to move back one space.
    • The first student to reach the “Finish” square wins the game.
  3. Discussion and reflection (15 minutes)
    • How did the game reinforce knowledge about the Western Ghats ecosystem?
    • Which questions were the most challenging, and why?
    • How can interactive learning tools like board games enhance ecological education?
  4. Geographic concepts: Biogeography, climatology, conservation geography, ecological relationships.
  5. Assessment
    • Participation in the game.
    • Accuracy of answers to ecosystem-related questions.
    • Engagement in post-game discussion and reflection.
  6. Differentiation
    • Create multiple versions of the game with varying difficulty levels.
    • Allow students to work in teams to answer questions.

Virtual coral island immersion: exploring India's reefs through 360° videos and mapping
While Virtual Reality (VR) offers a unique immersive experience, we can still achieve a powerful sense of exploration and understanding through readily available digital tools. In this activity, we will embark on a virtual journey of India's stunning coral island ecosystems, specifically focusing on the Lakshadweep and Andaman and Nicobar Islands. Using 360° videos, interactive maps, and online resources, we will analyze the biodiversity, geographical features, and conservation needs of these vital marine environments. This approach allows students to experience the beauty and complexity of these ecosystems, fostering a deeper connection and understanding, even without a dedicated VR equipment.

Activity: Students will virtually explore Indian coral island ecosystems using 360° videos and online resources to analyze their biodiversity, unique geographical features, and conservation needs.

Materials
• Computers or tablets with internet access
• Projector for whole-class viewing (optional, but recommended)
• 360° videos of Indian coral islands (see links below)
• Interactive maps of Indian coral islands (Google Maps)
• High-resolution images and videos of Indian coral reef organisms
• Worksheets with guided questions, map analysis tasks, and creative expression options

Procedure

  1. Introduction (15 minutes)
    • Begin with a discussion about the location and importance of Indian coral islands (e.g., Lakshadweep, Andaman and Nicobar Islands).
    • Discuss the unique geographical features and biodiversity of these islands.
    • Introduce the use of 360° videos and online maps as tools for virtual exploration.
  2. 360° video exploration (25 minutes)
    • Students will explore 360° videos of Indian coral reefs.
    • Here are some potential sources (always check for the most up-to-date links):
    • YouTube 360° Channels: Search for "Indian coral reefs 360°" or specific island names (e.g., "Lakshadweep 360°"). Channels like "The Ocean Agency" or National Geographic sometimes have relevant content.
    • National Geographic: The National Geographic website may have 360° videos or interactive content related to coral reefs.
    • Students can use their mouse or move their devices to look around the 360° environment.
    • Encourage students to observe the reef's structure, colours, and the diversity of marine life.
  3. Geographical context with Google Maps (15 minutes)
    Use Google Maps to provide a geographical context for the videos.
    Here's how
    Google Maps:
    • Search for specific Indian coral islands (e.g., "Andrott Island, Lakshadweep").
    • Use the satellite view to see the island's overall shape, location of reefs, and surrounding ocean.
    • Use street view (if available) to explore the island's coastal areas.
    • Students can analyze the relationship between the island's geography and coral reef ecosystem.
  4. Analysis and creative expression (20 minutes)
    Students complete worksheets with varied tasks:
    • Map analysis: Locate the islands, measure distances, and describe their geographical context.
    • Compare and contrast the coral reefs seen in different videos.
    • Research and describe a specific threat to these reefs (e.g., tourism impact, plastic pollution).
    • Create a short presentation or digital story about an Indian coral island.
  5. Class discussion and reflection (15 minutes)
    • Students share their findings and creations.
    • Discuss the importance of protecting Indian coral reefs.
    • Reflect on how virtual exploration tools enhance geographical understanding.
  6. Geographic concepts: Marine geography, island biogeography, coastal geomorphology, biodiversity, human-environment interaction, conservation geography, spatial technology.
  7. Assessment
    • Student engagement with 360° videos and Google Maps.
    • Accuracy and thoroughness of map analysis and worksheet responses.
    • Creativity and content of presentations or digital stories.
    • Participation in class discussions.
  8. Differentiation
    For students needing support:
    • Provide simplified maps with key features labelled.
    • Offer sentence starters or graphic organizers for written tasks.
    • Focus on a single island or a specific aspect of the ecosystem. For advanced learners:
    • Research the history of human settlement and land use on the islands.
    • Analyze the economic importance of coral reefs to the island communities.
    • Compare and contrast the challenges faced by different Indian coral island ecosystems.

Conclusion

When I first started teaching geography, I relied heavily on textbooks and diagrams, expecting students to grasp the complexity of ecosystems through words alone. But over time, I realized that geography isn’t something you just read about – it’s something you experience. The moment a student excitedly pointed out a tidal channel in the Sundarbans using Google Earth, or when a group passionately debated over a board game question about biodiversity in the Western Ghats, I knew I had found a better way to teach.

What I’ve learned is that students don’t just need to memorize concepts – they need to see, touch, question, and interact with them. A model of the Himalayan ecosystem makes the towering mountains and their climatic influence real. A comic strip on wetlands transforms abstract conservation efforts into a relatable story. A gamified lesson on desert ecosystems turns learning into an adventure rather than an obligation. These moments of engagement truly make geography come alive in the classroom.

As educators, we have the power to move beyond rote learning and create classrooms where students don’t just absorb knowledge – they explore it. These teaching aids are not just tools; they are invitations to students to think critically, make connections, and see themselves as part of the world they are studying. If we want students to understand ecosystems, we must let them experience them, even if it’s through a screen, a game, or a drawing. Because once they do, geography is no longer just a subject, it becomes a way of seeing the world.

Note: I gratefully acknowledge the guidance and encouragement of Ms. Sunita Matoo Principal of AMNS International School, Surat. Under her leadership I have been able to explore and implement innovative pedagogical practices in the classroom.

References

  • Google. (n.d.). Google Earth. Retrieved from https://www.google.com/earth/
  • Lee, J. J., & Hammer, J. (2011). Gamification in education: What, how, why bother? Academic Exchange Quarterly, 15(2), 146-151.
  • Ministry of Education. (2020). National Education Policy 2020. Government of India.
  • Myers, N., Mittermeier, R. A., Mittermeier, C. G., da Fonseca, G. A. B., & Kent, J. (2000). Biodiversity hotspots for conservation priorities. Nature, 403(6772), 853-858.
  • National Geographic Society. (n.d.). National Geographic MapMaker. Retrieved from https://education.nationalgeographic.org/resource/mapmaker/
  • Ramsar Convention. (n.d.). The importance of wetlands. Retrieved from https://www.ramsar.org/about/the-importance-of-wetlands
  • UNCCD. (n.d.). What is desertification? United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification. Retrieved from https://www.unccd.int/what-is-desertification
  • UNESCO. (n.d.). Sundarbans National Park. Retrieved from https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/798

Somnath Mukherjee is an A+ certified expert in the Development of Social Science Textbooks and Other Curricular Materials from NCERT, New Delhi. As a passionate postgraduate geography teacher at AMNS International School, Surat, he strives to ignite a love for geography among his students. He holds a gold medal in geography from the Regional Institute of Education, NCERT, Bhubaneswar. Beyond teaching, he immerses himself in geography literature and takes immense pride in classical dance, which remains a deep passion. He can be reached at bukunsomnath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Somnath Mukherjee
Teaching geography is about making ecosystems feel alive. Somnath Mukherjee shares some innovative tools to inspire curiosity and critical thinking in the classroom.

item.title: Making geography current
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Tapasya Saha

Current events make really good ‘teaching-learning materials’, for what better way is there to bring a subject alive in the classroom than observing and analyzing what is happening around you? Studying a subject through current events also makes it real for the students. The subject ceases to remain only in the pages of a textbook, instead students see it playing a part in their lives.

I have maintained a scrapbook with relevant news reports, maps, graphs, etc., to use in class. I will open a few pages of this scrapbook to show you how news events can become learning materials in geography classrooms.

Topic 1: Internal movements of the Earth – volcano and earthquake

  1. Volcano

News event* 1a

The photo taken on February 12, 2025, shows Mount Etna in Sicily erupting volcanic steam and gas. Tourists are risking their lives, getting closer, to take pictures.

Classroom process

The teacher can introduce volcanoes with this picture. She can mention how tourists are flocking to the site and enjoying the eruption. She can then move on to asking questions that will make the students remember, think, and draw logical inferences.

  • What does the picture show – an extinct, dormant, or active volcano?
  • Name a few countries that have active volcanoes.

Pointing to the picture, the teacher can explain that the shapes of volcanic mountains depend upon the fluidity of the magma.

  • Guess and draw the kind of upland features it would create if the magma that’s come out is a) very fluid b) moderately fluid and c) sticky (viscous).
  • Draw such volcanic relief features and colour them appropriately.
  • Find out how the Deccan Plateau in India was formed.
  • Mark the ‘ring of fire’ on a world map. Can you guess why it is called so?

The teacher can now explain the classification of volcanoes with examples, names, and map work.

B. Earthquake

News event* 1b

Two years ago on February 6, 2023, the people of Türkiye and Syria were jolted out of their sleep. At least 17,000 were killed with the numbers mounting within minutes as a great earthquake shook those countries in the early hours after 4 a.m. at 7.8 on the Richter. A second jolt came like a collaborator of the first, nine hours later destroying whatever buildings stood on or around the scene of the first trauma.

Classroom process

The teacher can talk about earthquakes along with volcanoes.

Some questions the teacher can ask to develop the “cause and effect” skill (in geography) amongst students can be:

  • Imagine a volcanic eruption taking place; what kind of movement would accompany such eruptions? Would such a movement always accompany volcanic eruptions?
  • Have you ever experienced an earthquake? If you have, describe your experience.
  • Imagine what would happen if the land under your feet was trembling and shaking. Try to draw what you would observe.
  • Is it right to say that every volcanic eruption can cause an earthquake, but every earthquake may not be associated with a volcanic eruption?
  • What would cause an earthquake and sometimes both earthquake and volcanic eruption?

Topic 2: Rise of the Himalayas and occurrence of earthquakes

News event*

The Indian Plate pressing onto the Eurasian Plate sculpted the Himalayas. The fault line involved runs right along the great Himalayan arc that stretches from Kashmir to the North East. It also implicates adjacent tracts in Pakistan, the Tibetan Autonomous Region of China, Nepal, and Bhutan.

This entire region is earthquake prone. The National Center for Seismology (Ministry of Earth Science) has divided India into five seismic Zones – I, II, III, IV, and V. The intensity is in ascending order.

The teacher can bring in ‘Plate Tectonic Theory’ to explain the rise of the Himalayas and explain the causes of earthquakes in the region.

The teacher can also discuss fault lines and get the class to talk about seismic zones and disaster management preparedness.

Topic 3: Off-shore minerals of India

News event*

Geological Survey of India gave an account of the different kinds of minerals that can be commercially extracted.
