After an amendment to the Offshore Areas Mineral (Development and Regulation) Bill, in 2023, 13 offshore areas of Gujarat, Kerala, and the Great Andaman and Nicobar Islands will start mining soon; the minerals are lime mud, construction sand, and polymetallic nodules.

Classroom process

Teachers mainly talk about minerals located under the surface of the Earth; using this news report we can learn about minerals under the sea on the continental shelf and along the coast. Some questions the teacher can raise in the class are:

-        Find out the major uses of the minerals listed above.

-        Which traditional occupation would be largely hit if coastal mining in these areas started operating? How?

-        Do you think the mining of these minerals would cause any harm to the coastal ecosystem?

-        Debate for and against the motion “Government should not encourage coastal mining of these minerals in the name of development”.

-        List the preparation governments should consider before any mining starts, on-shore or offshore.

-        Project work on “Offshore Mining in South India and Andaman and Nicobar Islands”.

Topic 4: Indian achievements in space exploration

News event

An image of the Chandrayaan-3 mission’s Vikram lander clicked about 15 m away by the Pragyan rover’s navigation camera. ISRO

Data brought by Chandrayaan

  • The Indian satellite Chandrayaan landed on the moon on August 23, 2023. It brought in a lot of data about the moon and confirmed the hypothesis that a primordial ocean of molten magma or lava is present under its surface, across the satellite. It also proved that the age of the south polar region of the moon is 3.7 billion years, just as when microbial life was emerging on Earth. The major parts of the moon are 4.53 billion years old.

Physical structure of the moon

  • The topmost layer of rocks on the moon on which all moon-landers have landed is called ‘regolith’. These are fine dust and small fragments resulting from frequent bombardments of meteorites that break into micro pieces. It is commonly called ‘moon dirt’. These bombardments also create numerous impact craters. Due to the airless and arid environment, there is no erosion of these craters. Hundreds of lunar missions from different parts of the Earth have left huge quantities of garbage on the regolith.

Similarities between the Earth and the moon

  • The Earth and the moon have the same evolutionary routes as indicated by the alignment of both bodies to the ecliptic plane of the solar system. Both their orbits and their tilts are similarly lined up. Both share a very similar isotopic composition of several elements like oxygen, silicon, titanium, etc. All these indicate a common origin from the coalescence of the same nebular dust.

Classroom process

Many of these latest Indian achievements may not have entered the textbook, but the teacher can always share developments and keep her students up to date and engaged.

While talking about the moon, the teacher can use the event of Chandrayaan landing on the moon. She can ask:

  • Which face of the moon do we always see?
  • Can you guess why it is so?
  • Where did the Chandrayaan land?
  • What is ‘moon dirt’? What is the other word for it?
  • When the south polar region of the moon was forming 3.75 billion years ago, what was happening on Earth?
  • How can you say that the Earth and the moon have the same origin?

Topic 5: Humans and climate change

An example of human factor (anthropogenic) for climate change – the Gangasagar story.

Perilously close: The Kapil Muni temple is just 470 metres from the sea: (right) Devotees trudge through marshy land to reach the confluence during the Gangasagar Mela on Sagar Island.

News event*

Sagar island stands at the confluence of the Hoogly river (a distributary of the Ganga) and the Bay of Bengal. Sagar island is part of the Sundarbans Delta. Kapil Muni’s Ashram stands here. The effect of climate change and the increase of temperature through carbon emissions is now clearly visible on the island. The sea was one kilo meter away from the temple just 10 years back, now it is hardly 470 meters from the temple. The cutting down of bushes and Sundari trees indiscriminately and removing the sand dunes have led to encroachment by the sea. Forests were cleared mainly to flatten the land for making space for bigger and grander melas during the holy dip in the confluence of river Ganga when lakhs of pilgrims from all over India come for a holy dip around Makar Sankranti. Rising sea water levels and more frequent occurrence of destructive cyclones are forcing the locals to leave their homes and let their cattle perish.

Classroom process on ‘human factor and climate change’

Understanding the anthropogenic factor in climate change and learning to act immediately should be everyone’s priority. The teacher can use this news report to ensure the responsibility students must undertake.

Some questions she can ask to lead the discussion can be:

  • What is meant by the term ‘climate change’?
  • In this case, how are the locals affected? Find out some more examples of human suffering due to climate change.
  • Explain what human activities are responsible for such developments.
  • Where is this place located?
  • How were the Sundari trees stopping the sea from encroaching the land?
  • What is the meaning of the term ‘confluence’?
  • On a map of India / world map mark the cities that are going to face similar situations in the near future due to climate change / rising sea levels.

Topic 6: Indian Agriculture

News event

Climate change can badly affect rabi crops in India. The situation during 2023-24 is clearly shown by the two graphs given below.

Unseasonal rainfall and unusual heat waves are the major reasons for crop failure. Graph 1 shows the main reason for crop damage during the 2023-24 rabi season.

The direct impact of climate change led to about 90% of the farmers to being more dependent on weather forecasts for their crop-sowing decisions amongst other factors. (Graph 2 shows the various other factors for their sowing decisions).

Effect of climate change on Indian agriculture

Like elsewhere, climate change is affecting agriculture in ways beyond our comprehension. The data of 2023-24 gives a vivid example of this. This news report shows the threat that hangs on Indian farmers. Sometimes the consequences of crop failures are very tragic.

Classroom process

Students can be given this entire paragraph, and Graphs 1 and 2. Then, the teacher can raise certain questions for discussion in the class.

  • Has climate change affected you? How?
  • Which cropping season is talked about here. Mention the months.
  • Name some crops that are grown during this cropping season.
  • Name the reasons for crop failure.
  • Why are 90% of the farmers dependent on weather forecasts for their sowing decisions?
  • What are some other factors for choosing the timing of sowing?
  • Collect data on the number of suicides committed by farmers due to crop failures. Discuss the problem and think of solutions.
  • The teacher can invite concerned officials and arrange an interview with the senior students.

*To see the original news reports, please see the bibliography. All news items have been modified for understanding the necessity of research on the news reports before using them in the class.

Bibliography

Topic 1. Internal Movements of the earth – volcano and earthquake

1a. Big Shot – The Hindu, February 25, 2025

1b. Quakes may well sharpen India’s seismic readiness. The Hindu, February 19, 2025

Topic 2. Rise of the Himalayas and the occurrence of earthquakes

Quakes may well sharpen India’s seismic readiness. The Hindu, February 19, 2025

Topic 3. Minerals of India

A battle between fishing and mining – The Hindu, March 3, 2025

Topic 4. Indian achievements in space exploration

First detailed map of moon’s south pole made from Chandrayaan data – The Hindu, February 25, 2025

Topic 5. Climate change

Pilgrims meet climate change on an island – The Hindu, February 14, 2025

Topic 6. Indian agriculture

Over 70% farmers still use cash to sell their produce’ – The Hindu, February 27, 2025

Tapasya Saha is a consultant at the Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. She has been a senior geography teacher for over 14 years. She can be reached at tapasya.saha@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tapasya Saha
Current events can be an invaluable resource for teaching geography. Tapasya Saha writes about how real-life events like earthquakes and volcanoes can help students relate with the concepts.

item.title: It’s geography, geography everywhere
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prof. Surendra Thakurdesai

People define geography in many ways, I define it as ‘organized commonsense’. To understand any geographical process, we only have to learn how to think in ‘the geography way’. And what is the ‘geography way’? It is our relationship with our surroundings, and also the behaviour of the elements on Earth. So there is human geography and physical geography. This makes geography omnipresent. You take any event and there will be some element of geography in it. So, almost everything that happens around us has a direct or indirect link with geography.

Geography has a two-way relationship with current events. The knowledge of geography helps us understand the causes and future possibilities related to an event, and each event when analyzed contributes to our knowledge of geographical processes. Take the huge landslides in Waynad, Kerala last monsoon, for instance. There were many geographical factors that contributed to the occurrence of this event – the natural angle of the slope, the extreme intense rainfall, modification in natural land use, etc. After the rescue and relief work was over, a detailed investigation was carried out, because of which we now have more information to decide the thresholds of processes associated with landslides in the Western Ghats. For example, that a cumulative rainfall of more than 700 mms without a break, rain intensity of 100 mm/hour, slope angle steeper than 70° can all lead to further landslides.

Crop failure is another event we often hear about. It could happen to any type of crop, whether farm crop, plantation, or orchard, and in any season. Farmers and agronomists believe that the reasons are loss of soil moisture, change in soil structure, break in rainfall or non-seasonal rainfall, frosty conditions, hail storms, etc. All these are aspects we learn in geography.

Collision of an oil tanker and a ship on the British coast, large flocks of killer whales coming ashore the Atlantic. Arabian Sea surface temperatures dropping sharply. When we zoom out, we realize that these are not standalone changes and that oceans in different parts of the world are experiencing changes. Beaches are getting polluted. In monsoon, litter comes ashore making beaches ugly; fish die of suffocation because of the plankton called Noctillia. All these are associated with the oceanographic processes. As geographers, we are well equipped to explain these events.

Unfortunately, this is a time of conflicts among different social, political, and cultural groups in various parts of the world. If we put all of them on a map, certain trends will begin to emerge. We will be able to notice that some of the conflicts, especially in warmer regions, are associated with climate related disasters like prolonged draughts, frequent floods, and cyclones. In other regions, it is the control over economically important resources that causes conflicts. It can be oil in the Middle East, rare minerals in Ukraine, or forests in Latin America. The list can go on. Rivers flowing between states is geography, but sharing of river water is a political issue. Political geography has gained utmost importance in the last few years. With rapid changes in governments, political identities, alignments, unification of economies, industry-driven economies growing larger than national economies, there is scope for geographers to interpret the underlying connections and also predict possible outcomes of these changes. Geo-strategically, important nations like India are at the pivot of these events. The expansion of the road network by China in Pakistan has geographic implications. A majority of the Asian countries do not have an all-weather port in the Indian Ocean. The Indian Ocean is important because more than half of the world’s population lives here. Thus, while sharing any news about Balochistan, Baltistan, or Hambantota we can discuss the multifold geography behind these places and events.

Every time we read or listen to some news event, a trained geographer can sense the attributes belonging to their own domain playing a crucial role in the event. As teachers we have to understand them and explain this to the students. How well a subject is accepted depends upon how relevant it is. Correlating geography to current events can highlight this relevance. It is for the teaching community to shine the spotlight on our subject.

Surendra Thakurdesai is Professor and Head, Department of Geography, R P Gogate and R V Jogalekar College (Autonomous), Ratnagiri, Maharashtra. He can be reached at surendratd@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prof. Surendra Thakurdesai
Geography matters because it's in the events we see daily, and it's all around us. Prof. Surendra Thakurdesai writes about how the study of geography helps understand real-world occurrences.

item.title: Geospatial data: transforming our understanding of the world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dipti Mukherji

Any data related to the Earth and its features is geographic or geospatial data. It helps you locate anything – whether a country or an address in a city using coordinates (longitudes and latitudes). Geospatial data also allows the discovery and exploration of the relationship between objects within the geographical space they occupy. This information can be used to understand interactions, patterns, and trends in a given space and make informed decisions. For example, there is an accident on a road. Traffic police can use geospatial data to find out the exact location of the accident, when it happened, and how. They can also mark similar accident spots and see why certain places are accident prone and take measures to avoid such incidents in the future.

History of geospatial mapping

By the late 18th and early 19th centuries, detailed maps were being produced to show individual properties, county boundaries, and demographics. But it was only in 1854 that it was discovered that geospatial data could also be used to solve problems facing humanity. The city of London was gripped by a cholera epidemic. The common belief at the time was that the disease was spreading by air. Dr. John Snow was a physician studying the epidemic and its spread. By locating the cholera deaths on a map of the city, Snow was able to conclude that the outbreak of the epidemic was due to contaminated water from a specific water pump. His map was unique because for the first time geographic data was used to solve a problem, in this case, medical. The value of such knowledge became evident and it led to the collection and storage of large volumes of geospatial data. By the end of the 20th century, with the rapid growth of computers and the internet, even the common man began using geospatial data to locate themselves, physical structures, natural objects, etc., on a map.

Where and how geospatial data can be applied

Geospatial data typically combines location information, which is usually the coordinates and attribute information, which are the characteristics of the object, event, or phenomena in a given location.

It is most useful when shared, analyzed, and used in combination with other data. It is visualized in the form of graphs, statistics, and cartograms. Insights that might be overlooked on a massive spreadsheet become obvious in easy-to-recognize visual patterns and images. This can make predictions faster, easier, and more accurate. The information about a location can either be static (the location of pine forests) or dynamic (the spread of an infectious disease).

Geospatial data is the interface between visualizing spatial data and non-spatial data (manmade objects, natural objects, and textual/numerical/categorical data) with geolocated coordinate accuracy. That is why:

  • Governments can take insights about health, disease, and weather, and better advice the public when a natural disaster strikes or an emergency health event occurs.
  • Electric and phone service providers can use data to help predict possible service disruptions and optimize maintenance and crew schedules.
  • Vegetation management can be carried out to assess water and moisture levels.
  • Meteorologists can work with incoming data to chart the path of climate related disasters that might be moving through an area.
  • Wildfire mapping is another new application for these technologies. This can be done using drones for aerial-mapping. This is important for inaccessible areas.

Geospatial data and future developments

Geospatial technology is becoming increasingly sophisticated, especially with the advent of machine learning and AI.

During the last decade, there has been a lot of progress in geospatial data management research. Here are five milestones that reflect the wide spectrum of this research.

  • There have been significant advancements in the field of 3D/4D geospatial databases. Spatiotemporal (space and time) data models including moving surface and solid geometries have been implemented in object-relational and object-oriented geospatial database management systems.
  • With the increasing popularity of web-mapping applications and the rapid growth of map data availability, maps based on geospatial data have become common with online map services such as Google Maps, Bing Maps, Open Street Maps, etc., as these processes use far fewer server resources.
  • Knowledge of data science is important for analyzing geospatial data, which is more often than not structured (organized in a predefined format such as rows and columns in a datasheet or lines or points on a map). It is easy to analyze structured data and therefore it is important to maintain the model of structured data. However, in future, geospatial data experts will also be able to recognize data and patterns from unstructured data, such as text messages, tweets, photos, or satellite imagery, which do not have a predefined format. These are not tabular but qualitative data. Therefore, methods for extraction of knowledge from unstructured data have to be improved.
  • The role of geospatial data management is to solve problems by bridging the gap between modern information technology concepts and geo-related sciences such as geography, geo-sciences, or civil engineering to provide tools and models to ease work across all disciplines. Future research will aim to introduce new approaches to the fundamental problem of multiple representation in geospatial data handling. These approaches will offer sophisticated modeling and visualization in a faster and more accurate mode.
  • Geospatial predictive analysis is a subset of geospatial data analysis and is one of the cornerstones of geospatial data science. By leveraging historical data, real-time spatial data, and cutting-edge algorithms, it allows us to anticipate future trends and make informed decisions. Machine learning algorithms can quickly analyze large datasets of geographic information to identify patterns and make predictions. Whether predicting disease outbreaks, traffic congestion, or crop yields, the applications of predictive analysis in spatial data science are vast.

Conclusion

Geospatial technology revolves around the use of geospatial data with a geographical or spatial component. This technology includes GIS (Geographic Information Systems), GPS (Global Positioning System), remote sensing, and other techniques that allow us to map, analyze, and interpret location-based data. It is best for measuring, mapping, monitoring, and managing data. Initially, it catered mostly to specialists in geography or cartography, but today the integration of geospatial tech with coding and data science has made it accessible to a broader range of industries and professions. Data can be visualized to understand patterns such as incidences of crime in a locality or cases of malaria in a region, which will help us make decisions regarding the eradication of malaria or to find the causes of crime in a city.

At present, interactive mapping is becoming very popular. If anyone wants to search for houses with schools, clinics, and markets nearby, it is possible through geospatial data. JavaScript, Python, and Application Programming Interfaces offer opportunities that make spatial data accessible and useful for everyday decision-making. During COVID-19, geospatial data helped track the spread of the virus and predict hotspots. One of the most promising applications of geospatial data and technology is the development of smart cities. The future of geospatial technology promises more user-friendly interfaces that will make these tools of data analysis accessible to everyone and then geospatial data analysis-based studies are bound to become an even more integral part of our daily lives. For developers of this technology the opportunity lies in how to harness these tools to build interactive, location-based solutions. Coding with geospatial data is the new way of seeing and understanding the world.

Get your students involved

Help your students understand geospatial data science by getting them to do the following activities.

Map your school or neighbourhood (Beginner)

Goal: Understand basic spatial data and mapping
Tools: Paper maps, Google My Maps
Activity:

  • Students collect simple location data (e.g., where they live, places they visit).
  • Plot these points on a map.
  • Analyze patterns (e.g., how far people travel to school, clustering of homes).

2. Story map creation (Intermediate)

Goal: Combine narrative with geospatial visualization
Tools: Google Earth Web
Activity:

  • Students create an interactive story around a historical event, local issue, or environmental concern.
  • Add text, photos, and spatial data to build an engaging map-based story.

3. Environmental analysis using remote sensing (Advanced)

Goal: Learn about satellite imagery and land use classification
Tools: Google Earth Engine
Activity:

  • Examine changes in land cover over time (e.g., deforestation, urbanization).
  • Discuss the impacts on local ecosystems or climate.

4. Spatial data collection and analysis (Intermediate – Advanced)

Goal: Introduce spatial data collection and GIS analysis
Tools: Mobile apps like Survey123 or Google Forms with location enabled
Activity:

  • Collect data on something like tree types on campus, trash locations, or Wi-Fi strength.
  • Import data into the app you have and analyze spatial trends or patterns.

5. Population density and urban planning simulation (Intermediate)

Goal: Understand how data informs urban development
Tools: QGIS or a simple spreadsheet + maps
Activity:

  • Analyze population density maps.
  • Propose locations for new schools, parks, or stores based on data.
  • Justify decisions using spatial reasoning and demographic information.

Dipti Mukherji is a retired professor in geography from the Department of Geography, University of Mumbai. She has extensive teaching and research experience. She also conducts geo map quiz for school kids. She can be reached at diptimukherji@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dipti Mukherji
Geographic data helps locate places in a country or city. It can even help understand the outbreak of epidemics. Dipti Mukherji shares other ways this domain can be useful.

item.title: Underwater wonders
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr Joji V S

Mid Oceanic Ridges (MORs) are the longest and largest mountain ranges on Earth with 90 percent of the ranges lying underwater. MORs are formed when the Earth’s tectonic plates move apart allowing the magma beneath the Earth’s crust to rise up producing enormous volcanic eruptions of basalt. The speed at which the plates move away from each other affects the shape of the ridge – slower movement results in mountains with steep, irregular topography, while faster movement produces mountains with wider profiles and gentler slopes. Tensional fractures and block faulting are common in MORs and magnetic anomaly patterns are their most striking feature.

Mid-oceanic ridges around the world

At nearly 65000 kilometers long, the MORs encircle the entire globe, and while they are mostly found in the Indian, Atlantic, and Pacific Oceans, they are present in all five oceans of the world. MORs are crucial for understanding the theory of plate tectonics and the formation of the Earth's crust. Here, we take a look at some of the more popular ridges across the world.

The Atlantic Ocean

Found in the Atlantic ocean, the Mid Atlantic Ridge (MAR) runs 333 km south of the North Pole up to the Bouvet Island and is one of the most extensively studied MORs in the world. Marie Tharp and her colleague Bruce Heezen, were the first to discover the MAR and prove the theory of continental drift. The ridge bisects the North and South Atlantic basins, hence the origin of the name 'mid-ocean ridge'.

The northern part of the Mid Atlantic Ridge is called the Dolphin Ridge and the southern part is called the Challenger Ridge. The MAR features a deep rift valley, volcanic activity, and hydrothermal vents. The MAR exhibits a crustal age gradient, with the youngest crust near the ridge crest.

The Indian Ocean

The MORs in the Indian Ocean are Investigator Ridge, Central Indian Ridge, Southwest Indian Ridge, Southeast Indian Ridge, 90º East Ridge, Carlsberg Ridge, and Murray Ridge. These ridges are formed by slow tectonic plate movements. The Central Indian Ridge, Southwest Indian Ridge, and Southeast Indian Ridge converge at the Rodriguez Triple Junction, forming an inverted Y-shaped ridge system. They are characterized by high heat flow, volcanic activity, and topographic variations.

The Pacific Ocean

The MORs in the Pacific Ocean are East Pacific Rise and Gorda Ridge. These are characterized by fast seafloor spreading, resulting in a relatively broad and less rugged topography compared to the slower-spreading ridges. These ridges are sites of volcanic activity, producing new oceanic crust and often feature hydrothermal vents.

Features of mid-oceanic ridges

Heat flow in the Earth’s crust

The movement of heat from the Earth's interior to the surface is the primary energy flow that is responsible for the planet's dynamic nature, including plate tectonics and volcanic activity. Heat is generated in the Earth’s crust from radioactive decay in the upper crust and mantle and external heat mainly contributed by the sun in the form of electromagnetic radiation (EMR), which transfers into the Earth by conduction, convection, and radiation. The heat flow on the surface of the Earth is maximum at the axis of the MOR and decreases as it moves away from the axis (i.e. towards the flanks of MOR), which is why MORs are characterized by frequent volcanism and earthquakes with high heat flow values.

Sedimentation on the surface of the Earth

Sedimentation is the process of deposition of rock fragments, soil, organic matter, or dissolved material that has been eroded or transported by various geomorphic agents like wind, waves, and currents in oceans, seas, rivers, and glaciers, or gravity. The sedimentation rate is different at different places on Earth depending upon energy conditions with respect to motion or position like kinetic and potential energies. It is minimum at the axis of the MORs and increases slightly as one moves away from the axis towards the flanks. Sedimentation rate is highest at deep-sea trenches.

Palaeo-magnetic anomaly pattern

The palaeo-magnetic anomaly pattern is considered as the most significant characteristic feature of MORs. When new magmatic eruptions occur at the axis of MORs, magnetic elements or minerals of basaltic volcanism align parallel to the magnetic field of the Earth. There are two types of palaeo-magnetic anomaly patterns:

  • Positive or normal magnetic anomaly pattern – magnetic elements align parallel to the present magnetic field of the Earth.
  • Negative or reverse magnetic anomaly pattern – magnetic elements align in the reverse direction to the Earth’s magnetic field.

Black Smokers

Black smokers are cracks or fractures in the MORs, which appear as black, chimney-like structures that emit what appears to be a cloud of black smoke but is actually water. Black smokers are formed when seawater seeps down into the Earth's crust, is heated by magma, and then rises back to the seafloor through the fractures. Black smokers emit particles with high levels of sulfur-bearing minerals, like sulfides of iron, copper, zinc, and lead. Black smokers usually attain temperatures above 400°C. This water is rich in dissolved minerals from the crust, most notably sulfides. Black smokers were first reported in 1979 on the East Pacific Rise by scientists from Scripps Institution of Oceanography during the RISE Project. Black smokers are found in the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, at an average depth of 2,100 m. There are also white smoker vents, but they emit less density minerals like those containing barium, calcium, and silicon.

Hydrothermal Alteration

Hydrothermal alteration or change is a very common feature of the mid-oceanic ridges and is due to the circulation of seawater through the newly formed oceanic crust. Hydrothermal alteration or change modifies crustal rocks as they interact with the hot seawater, leading to changes in their mineralogy and chemistry. Serpentinization is a very common hydrothermal alteration in MORs.

Student activities

1. Crayon tectonics

Materials: Paper, crayons, cardboard

Activity:

  • Have students draw symmetrical patterns with crayons on two pieces of paper (representing the ocean floor).
  • Slowly slide the papers apart over a slit in a cardboard box to mimic seafloor spreading.
  • They’ll see how new crust forms and patterns mirror each side of the ridge.

Concepts: Seafloor spreading, symmetry of magnetic stripes

2. Clay mid-ocean ridge model

Materials: Modelling clay or Play-Doh in different colours

Activity:

  • Use different colours to layer "magma" in the center and "older crust" on the sides.
  • Simulate the spreading of tectonic plates by pulling apart the clay ridge.

Concepts: Plate tectonics, creation of new crust at divergent boundaries

3. Chocolate divergence demo

Materials: Kit-Kats or layered chocolate bars

Activity:

  • Break the chocolate bar in the middle to simulate two tectonic plates pulling apart.
  • Show how "magma" (the gooey inside) can come up in the middle.

Concepts: Divergent plate boundaries, magma formation

4. Interactive map exploration

Tools: Google Earth or tectonic map printouts

Activity:

  • Have students explore mid-ocean ridges like the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
  • Ask them to locate major ridges and note which continents are moving apart.

Concepts: Global distribution of mid-ocean ridges, plate movement

5. Puzzle plate tectonics

Materials: World map puzzle or cut-outs of continents

Activity:

  • Let students fit continents together like a puzzle (especially South America and Africa).
  • Use the Mid-Atlantic Ridge to explain how they drifted apart over millions of years.

Concepts: Continental drift, evidence for seafloor spreading

6. Story of a Rock

Activity:

  • Ask students to write a short story from the point of view of a rock forming at a mid-ocean ridge.
  • Include how it's formed, moves away, and possibly gets subducted (or sinks beneath another plate).

Concepts: Rock cycle, movement of crust, age of seafloor

Dr. Joji is Associate Professor and Head, Department of Marine Geology and Geophysics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin. He can be reached at jojivsdeepam@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr Joji V S
From the Indian Ocean to the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, the world of mid-oceanic ridges is a mysterious place. Dr Joji explains the science behind some of these longest mountain chains hidden deep under the sea.

item.title: Biogeographic zones of India: A bounteous land
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mamata Pandya

While we live in a country of abundant natural wealth, we also live in a time when this priceless heritage is severely threatened. Sadly, it is human activities that are rapidly endangering these resources across the different biogeographic zones. What are biogeographic zones? These are geographical areas with distinct environmental conditions that support unique plant and animal life. There are 10 biogeographic zones in India. The study of these zones is called biogeography. Biogeography reminds us that a country is defined not just by its political borders, but also by the wealth of its natural features and resources.

Construction of hydro-power dams, roads, railways, industries, poaching, and encroachment of forests and wetlands are seriously threatening these natural habitats and their flora and fauna. The reduction in the forest cover is threatening not only wildlife, but also vital ecological services like soil, water, temperature regulation, as well as traditional livelihoods and lifestyles. Pollution, oil spills, sewage, and effluent disposal, dredging and mining of shore sand leading to erosion and flooding, overexploitation of fishery resources are all a threat to coasts and islands. In addition to these, climate change is having a critical impact on all the biogeographic regions. The increased frequency and severity of natural disasters, such as floods and landslides, is impacting natural ecosystems and geography, as well as affecting the lives and livelihoods of people. Climate change impacts like sea-level rise, increase in water temperature, changes in ocean currents, and coral bleaching are affecting marine ecosystems.

We need to join hands to conserve and protect this priceless heritage and we can start by getting to know a little more about them.

Let’s take a cross-country train ride to get a small taste of the feast of sights, sounds, and experiences that these different zones offer.

Climb aboard the Biogeography Express!

A magical journey

Let’s take a trip on an imaginary train
Experience a journey you will want to take again and again.
Let’s travel across a magical land
Of mountains and valleys, plateaus, and sand.


Biogeography, biogeography
It’s all about regions and variety
That marks not just the political geography
But also the geology, botany, zoology, and ecology.


In forests and fields, deserts and seas
Animals and crops, microbes and trees.
Each unique to the landscape and terrain
Some like it dry, and others thrive in rain.


Here on the wonderful Biogeography Express
Explore and discover, and experience afresh.
Colours and patterns, functions and form
Of creatures that fly, swim, leap, and swarm.


Let’s start our journey from the lofty Trans-Himalaya, up high
Snow-clad peaks, marshlands, high-altitude lakes, blue sky.
Snow leopards, yaks, and double-humped camels


The Himalayan cold desert is home to these mammals.
Shining blue lakes in the rugged landscape
Welcome winged visitors of many sizes and shapes.


The Himalaya is expansive in range and terrains
Soaring altitudes, gentler gradients, and moraines
An ancient mountain system, original home to many now living
Pure wild breeds of sheep and goats, and their domesticated kin.
Here we wander with shepherds and their wooly flocks
Up snowy mountains to alpine pastures, down valleys, and over rocks.


Biogeography, biogeography
It’s about topography and biodiversity.


Where the mighty Ganga flows is the Gangetic Plain.
Here river dolphins swim, and gharials are found,
Regal tigers prowl, and deer abound.
The fertile plains offer bounteous yields
From forests and farmlands and fields.
A plenitude of plants for food and for health
And a multitude of people add to the wealth.


Biogeography, biogeography
See it, smell it, taste it, feel it!


The North East is truly a Garden of Eden
Full of priceless treasures, many still hidden.
Feathery ferns, bright orchids, and bamboos tall
Where rhinos roam and Hoolock Gibbons call.
A vibrant mosaic of languages and faces
Music, dance and feasts of many races.


Biogeography, biogeography
Appreciate it, study it, learn from it!


Savannah is the vegetation of the Semi-arid lands
Here legumes and shrubs thrive in expansive grasslands.
To the myriad wetlands come birds, many more than you think
Cranes, pelicans, geese, and thousands of flamingos pink.


Discover that Deserts seem dry, but they are so alive
Their dwellers have special tricks to survive
Store water, shed leaves, or burrow in the sands.
Why even tigers, and lions and leopards roar in these lands.


Biogeography, biogeography
Treasure it, value it, protect it!


Walk through the Western Ghats, and do it all
Climb hills, sail gushing rivers, gasp at a thundering waterfall.
Meet a tahr, a Toda*, and a tiger too
Jumbos in forests, and a hornbill or two!
Colourful frogs that croak and call,
Snakes and snails that slither and crawl.


Biogeography, biogeography
Learn from it, be enriched by it.


The Deccan Peninsula has on offer all of the best
Highlands and grasses, plateau and forest.
Trees, flowers, bamboo, and grasses many more
Buffaloes, cows, goats, and sheep galore.
Celebrate the seeds and cereals upon which we feast
With Gonds, Bhils and Warli, and their dancing feet.


Biogeography, biogeography
We can plant in it, grow in it, and feast from it!

Take a walk along the Coasts, on a sandy shore
Or dive into the sea to further explore.
Meet crabs, crocs, and tigers in a mangrove tangle.
Encounter clown fish and anemone in a coral jungle.


Sail to the Islands lying in the sea blue and deep
East to the Andamans and westwards to Lakshadweep
In the Andaman and Nicobar, so lush and green
See a megapode, a wild pig, or a Nicobar pigeon preen.
Coconut, breadfruit, and banana thrive in the fertile soil of the Lakshadweep
The aquatic waters equally rich with corals, seagrasses, and seaweed.


Biogeography, Biogeography
Track it, document it, delight in it!


Biogeography, biogeography
It’s all about regions and variety
This diversity is vital for every part of all life
It’s fragile, it’s threatened, and it’s on the edge of a knife.


Biogeography, Biogeography
Celebrate it, respect it, conserve it!


*The Toda are a predominant tribe residing in the Nilgiri Hills of Tamil Nadu.

Mamata Pandya worked at the Centre for Environment Education at Ahmedabad for over three decades, where she was engaged in developing creative teaching-learning resources for educators and children. She is now an independent consultant, editor, writer, translator, storyteller, and blogger. She can be reached at mamata.pandya@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mamata Pandya
India’s natural wealth is vast and varied. Mamata Pandya’s poem is a lyrical journey across the country’s biogeographic zones, threats that plague them, and the need for conservation.

item.title: Geography for economic development
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
M V Srinivasan

“Geography sets the stage upon which economic activities unfold.” – R.M. Montgomery

Recently, BRICS, an association of five large nations of the world – Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa – began discussing the introduction of a potential BRICS common currency to reduce reliance on the American dollar. Although no concrete plans have yet been formed, the idea has been resisted by the United States of America. Why? What was it that drove countries in Europe to form the European Union (EU) in 1993? Within Asia, countries came together to form the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC) and even establish a university – South Asian University – to strengthen regional consciousness and leadership. In West Asia, oil producing countries came together to form the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC). All these economic developments require the appreciation and understanding of geography. In this article, I will highlight how economic development is connected to a country’s geography and how the study of geography will help us better understand and appreciate economics.

Geography and the factors of production: In economics, factors of production – land, labour, capital, entrepreneurship – is an important fundamental concept. The class 9 NCERT economics textbook has a lesson titled The Story of Palampore, which discusses how the four factors of production enable various agricultural and nonagricultural activities in the village of Palampur. If a teacher begins this lesson by introducing the physical features of Palampur – that the village resides in the Indo-Gangetic or Northern Plains, and therefore is a fertile alluvial region, has abundant water resources, and favourable climatic conditions for agriculture – students will be able to see the connection between geographical factors and the economic concept of factors of production.

Geography and GDP: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is another important topic in economics. The NCERT economics textbook for class 10, Understanding Economic Development, in a lesson titled Sectors of the Indian Economy discusses how a country's GDP is estimated. Various sectors such as (i) the primary sector, which includes agriculture and allied sectors such as animal husbandry, fishing, poultry, mining and quarrying, (ii) the secondary sector, which includes manufacturing industries and (iii) the tertiary sector, which includes banking, hotels and restaurants, transport and communication contribute to a country’s  GDP . In the class 10 NCERT geography textbook, topics such as agriculture, mines and minerals, manufacturing industries, transport and communication are discussed. If the lesson on GDP is preceded by an understanding of these geographical concepts, students will see how geography contributes to a country’s GDP.

Geography and trade: For centuries, countries have been trading with each other via the waterways – oceans, seas, and rivers. Students may be encouraged to study world maps and identify countries to which India exports and countries from which India imports. If they mark probable trade routes, they will be able to appreciate the relevance of geography. Maps will tell students that the most economically developed countries are those that have long coastlines. Trade is a challenge for landlocked countries as buying and selling has to happen via land and passing through different countries means paying taxes, producing different documents, following and accepting the rules of the countries on the trading path.

Geography and industry clusters: Ask students if they have ever noticed entire streets lined by a particular kind of service, for example shops that sell and repair electronic equipment, or streets with shops that sell a particular kind of product like jewellery shops, or how educational institutions and coaching centres often nestle close to each other. Geographic locations contribute to the formation of industry clusters allowing material providers, designers, producers, and sellers to feed off each other and contribute to economic growth.

There are many such examples that one can identify to show the geography-economics link. For instance, why is wheat cultivated in the northern and central parts of India and not in the south? Besides soil, climatic conditions play an important role in farming practices. Why are apples grown in hilly regions? An increase in the GDP is considered as the first indicator of the economic prosperity of a country and this is largely dependent on a country’s wise use of its geographic features as we have seen in the above examples.

M.V. Srinivasan is Professor of Economics, Department of Education in Social Sciences, NCERT, New Delhi. He can be reached at vadivel.srinivasan@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
M V Srinivasan
Geography plays a crucial role in a country's economic growth. Srinivasan Vadivel writes about how natural features influence sectors like agriculture, industry, and trade.

item.title: Doing historical geography in the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Deepika Gupta

Distinctions between the disciplines of geography and history are often treated as impermeable in most schools in India. Entirely distinct and distanced from one another, the possibility of them overlapping is rare. While school history takes the responsibility of teaching about political, social, and cultural processes of the past, geography concerns itself solely with the study of mountains, forests, rivers, climate, and mineral resources to name a few. However, the boundaries between the two disciplines are more fluid than what is usually imagined in the school curriculum and pedagogical practices.

The disciplines of geography and history are conjoined in dealing with the relationships between people and their environment. History cannot be studied without referring to geography. Climate, agriculture, or availability of resources have the potential to alter the course of political or military events and socio-cultural processes. Similarly, all geography is historical. Studying geography without reference to history would imply that one assumes the presence of an eternally fixed landscape. This approach does not account for the changes (physical and human) that happen over time. This is a fatal error that threatens to reduce the study of geography to empirical descriptions in a state of timelessness. On the other hand, it is important to remember that the elements of time and human action are as important to geography as they are to history.

Through the example of voyages of exploration undertaken by Europeans from the mid-15th century, in this article, I will explore the ways in which geography can be taught historically by situating the complex processes of human interaction with their environment on a timescale.

The voyages of exploration

In the middle of the 15th century, Europeans began setting out on expeditions. Their focus was on finding new routes rather than a search to find new lands. Their motivations were political, economic, and intellectual. They were inspired by the possibility of expanding their political power and military influence, finding new sea routes for profitable trade as well as satisfying their curiosity about the world.

The period of explorations began with the Portuguese exploring the coast of Africa. Henry the Navigator, the prince of Portugal, patronized these early voyages and set in motion the processes of colonization and transatlantic slave trade. By the end of the century, Vasco de Gama had reached India by sailing around the Cape of Good Hope and Christopher Columbus had claimed to have reached the East by sailing westward (while he had only reached the American continent). Years later, Columbus’ claim was debunked. Ferdinand Magellan, a Portuguese navigator, set out on an expedition with his team that circumnavigated the world and demonstrated that Columbus had discovered a new continent that was separated from the East Indies by an ocean. Even after America was declared as a new continent, the question remained: was there a great landmass somewhere in the southern oceans? This legend of the existence of a huge landmass was demolished in the second half of the 18th century when Captain James Cook, an English navigator, landed in New Zealand and then reached Australia but could not find the great continent of the South Pacific, as had been imagined by many.

The search for new routes led Europeans to new lands and set in motion the process of colonization. However, in addition to the politico-economic changes brought about by the explorations, they contributed towards an enhanced understanding of the world, including land, sea, flora, fauna, and societies. They were crucial in advancing navigational techniques and map-making. The magnetic compass was used extensively, and latitudes were measured using improved instruments and techniques. In contrast to the technical advances that were made, determining the longitude continued to pose challenges as it required time keeping and there were no portable and accurate time-keeping devices at the time.

It is interesting to think about the voyages in this light. They led to advances in the understanding of the physical as well as human geography. New lands and cultures were encountered. Despite this, it comes as a surprise that the voyages are taught exclusively as the subject-matter of the discipline of history. The focus is on the European encounters with indigenous people, political conflicts and colonization, and exploitation and slavery. On the other hand, latitudes, longitudes, and contemporary maps are taught as exclusive domains of geography.

Geography and history: porous and permeable

Geography and history are constituents of a broader subject of social science. However, despite curricular recommendations to adopt interdisciplinary and integrated approaches, they are taught separately from one another. The notion is: geography concerns itself with empirical descriptions of the present and history remains focused on understanding the past. As a result, voyages are confined to the domain of history in school teaching and are never mentioned in the geography classroom. This is despite their importance in shaping the knowledge of geography as we know it today.

In the 1950s, H.C. Darby, a geographer, argued that there was a strong element of geography in doing history and several historians were giving geographical introductions to their works. Simultaneously, he also argued in favour of the element of history in geography. According to him, the present-day geography is in the process of becoming history, hence ‘all geography is historical geography, either actual or potential.’ His argument about the porous boundaries between the two disciplines offers scope for interdisciplinarity and opens the pathway to several pedagogical possibilities. One way of bringing history and geography together is through historical geography.

Doing historical geography through cartography

Knowledge in school geography is treated as static information. It is considered as given and stagnant, something that has always existed. Doing historical geography provides a way out of this timelessness. Historical geography implies viewing the environmental phenomenon of the present with reference to the human activities and geological processes in the past. It is important for a geographer to explore the question: why does a landscape look the way it does today? For instance, a landscape that one finds barren today might have been a fertile agricultural field several years ago and was exhausted due to excessive agricultural use. Humans have been historical actors in shaping the world as we know it.

The previous example of voyages of exploration fits perfectly in the domain of historical geography. It frames the discipline of geography in a temporal context and throws light on the ways in which human knowledge about oceans, land, and continents has been created through a consistent series of efforts over time. Understanding the history of map making is one aspect of geographical knowledge that is historical and has developed through human effort and sustained enterprise over a long period of time.

Humans have been making maps since 600 BCE. The intent behind these early initiatives was to represent their view of the world rather than creating maps to scale. Ptolemy’s map of the world, produced in the second century CE, has been considered as the moment heralding the birth of cartography. He devised a way to represent the three-dimensional globe on a two-dimensional surface and used latitudes and longitudes to develop a coordinate system. This was an advancement in cartography (the science of drawing maps), but people, in what would later be called the ‘Old World’, were still very far from understanding the world. They did not know about the existence of a continent to the west of Europe, separating it from Asia; neither did they know about a continent in the south Pacific.

The voyages of exploration, as elaborated upon in the previous section, led to strides in the mapping of the world. Around 1500 CE, almost 13 centuries after Ptolemy, Juan de la Cosa, a Spanish cartographer travelling with Columbus, produced a map of the world in which, for the first time, both North America and South America were depicted. As navigators and sailors embarked on further explorations, nautical maps were produced with greater precision. Today, we owe a great deal of our knowledge of the world to these voyages.

Although maps are a part of this long historical tradition of human struggle to understand the world, they are treated as timeless objects in the classrooms. Map work in the classroom involves learners plotting an industrial or mineral site, a mountain, a river, or a city on a blank map. This act does not facilitate the learner to understand why a map is even important in the first place. It is mechanical. Hence, it is important to bring a historical perspective to a geography classroom by placing maps under a historical lens. The learning experience would become exciting if the learners were made to ponder upon the history of cartography and the processes through which present-day maps were made possible. In this way, they would be able to grasp the reason why maps are important. Thinking along this history would make learners wonder about the human enterprise in knowing about the world and how humans interacted with the environment in the past. It would make the experience of learning geography more dynamic, interactive, and stimulating.

However, one must stay cautious of falling down the slippery slope of looking at history for every geographical study of the present. It can be exhausting to find historical traces of everything one studies in the present. Moreover, it threatens to marginalize the study of geography itself by turning it into a full-scale study of history. To avoid this, geography teaching could retain focus on the present while simultaneously drawing insights from the past to understand the historical evolution of landscapes and the development of geographical tools such as maps. The element of historicity would draw geography learners towards thinking in terms of time, in addition to space. The aim is not to have a domineering effect wherein one discipline overpowers the other or to shatter their disciplinary identities altogether. On the contrary, the idea is to borrow from the other discipline without imposition. By bringing the two disciplines together in a harmonious interaction, the teacher will be able to create a classroom ethos that would foster interdisciplinarity and encourage the learners to reflect along multiple dimensions.

Deepika Gupta is a PhD researcher at Ca’ Foscari University of Venice, Italy and a former school history teacher. Her areas of interest are pedagogy of history, Adivasi studies, curriculum research, and sociology of education. She can be reached at deepika2994@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Deepika Gupta
Geography and history are treated as distinct subjects, but what if they were taught together? Deepika Gupta explores how blending these two disciplines can enrich students' understanding of both the past and the present.

item.title: A dip in the Mahakumbh
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sheel

It’s the 1st of February, 2025. I’m at the riverside in Prayagraj, enticed here by the heavy advertising (“once in 144 years”) and the constant reporting and images of swarms of people taking a dip at the Mahakumbh.

An air of mysticism hovers over the riverbanks, where apart from people like me who have come here purely to witness the spectacle, are throngs of sadhus and saints and other faithful. Religious chants and bells ring out, devotional songs play on loudspeakers, and fascinating rituals that I cannot fully fathom are in progress. Now and then, large processions pass before my wondering eyes, some with spiritual gurus mounted on colourfully bedecked, glittering chariots built over small trucks, and a few actually drawn by horses.

At the heart of it all is the Triveni Sangam, a place of pilgrimage for Hindus. The consideration that has attracted millions here is a matter of faith – “A dip at this holy place will wash away all your karma.” While this is believed of many other temples and rivers in India, confluences (sangam) are seen as particularly sacred, this one being the most hallowed of all.

Voluble boatmen declaim as they ferry people to this place, “See the two differently coloured waters flowing together side by side, slowly mixing together? The greenish one is the Yamuna, which comes from the direction of Delhi, Mathura, and Agra, and this other one is the Ganga, which is born as the Bhagirathi at Gangotri and passes Rishikesh, Haridwar, Kanpur.”

The tone turns fairly dramatic mid-speech – “There is also a third river which we cannot see – it is the Saraswati, and it joins these two rivers from below the ground. That’s why this sangam is called the Triveni Sangam.”

“Many people say that Saraswati doesn’t exist, but she does. There is a well in the Akbar fort some way upstream from here – they say Akbar built it, but there is an Ashokan pillar there. How did it get there? The truth is that that place is much older than Akbar!” Is the indignation an act? I muse, not quite sure of the truth.

“The fort is in the hands of our army now, but that well…. Scientists tested its water some years ago and came to the conclusion that the water in it was neither that of the Ganga nor of the Yamuna. The scientists poured some colours in the well, and they found those coloured waters joining the Ganga and Yamuna here at the Sangam! So tell me, then how did the water get there? It is the Saraswati flowing below the ground!” The ending is triumphant.

I cannot help but notice the concomitance of geography, history, and culture, with an admixture of science (pseudoscience?) as well as myth here.

***

River basins have been the cradles of civilization – consider Mesopotamia (the word means ‘the land between the rivers’ in Greek!), Egypt, or the Indo-Saraswati civilization. Before the industrial age, rivers offered more possibilities of travel and transportation than overland routes, and confluences were the convergences of two waterways. They significantly expanded the possibilities for trade and commerce, and facilitated cultural exchange, which made them important places. Cities and towns sprung up at such sites.

Confluences are usually places of great scenic beauty (especially where people have left them untouched) and also places where the enormous power of water is clearly evidenced. They often invoke a sense of wonder. In the mountains, one frequently gets to see the sweet beauty of small streams flowing down mountains, which turn into energy-filled torrents as water falls off cliffs, eventually joining a larger creek or river rushing across the valley floor. The tenor of the river often changes as it hits plain land, the course being determined both by the terrain and by gravity. From bubbling and frothing over rocky terrain, rivers begin to flow more lazily as they widen out, sometimes appearing so still that the flow is all but invisible. When two large rivers merge, as at the Triveni Sangam, the sheer volume and spread of water can be awe-inspiring and humbling. The utter insignificance of the individual human being hits you hard!

At many confluences, the difference in the colour of the waters of the combining rivers also makes for a vibrant picture. The colour is a result of the water eroding the land, and collecting not just soil but also other matter as it passes by forests and towns. In some places, though, changes wrought by human beings appear to have controlled nature, as at the confluence of the Danube with the Ilk and the Inn at Passau in Germany. The rivers are embanked by concrete walls and landscaped parks as if they were mere channels. In India, the embankments are generally filled with temples and man-made ghats, attesting to the symbolism of the confluence as a site of spirituality.

One interesting aspect of confluences is the loss of identity of rivers. Often, the smaller river which drains into the larger one is designated as the tributary of the latter and loses its name. Sometimes, it is the other way round, and in some cases, both rivers might lose their names and take on a new one entirely. Take the case of the Ganga, for instance. The river has two main headstreams, the Alaknanda and the Bhagirathi. Four smaller headstreams, the Dhauliganga, Nandakini, Pindar, and Mandakini, are subsumed into the Alaknanda before it meets the Bhagirathi at Devprayag. From here, the river flows forward with a different name – the Ganga! Downriver, when the Yamuna (or other tributaries such the Gandak, Gomti, Ghagra, etc.) join it, they too lose their individual names and become part of the Ganga.

To the faithful, such merging often has the spiritual connotation of communion/merging with the divine/universe. In India, some part of the conviction in the powers of confluences springs from this aspect. The religious significance of the literal churning of the waters as two streams join together is reminiscent of the myth of the churning of the oceans to obtain the ‘Kumbh,’ the pot of immortalizing nectar, or of becoming one with the divine.

Lending further credence to people’s faith and beliefs are the many myths of rivers, such as the legend of King Bhagirath who is fêted for bringing the Ganga to the Earth so that his ancestors’ sins could be washed away. While the dip at Prayagraj is considered the most potent in this matter, the “karma will be washed away” refrain can certainly be heard at other confluences as well, for instance the confluence of the Narmada and the Kaveri (not to be confused with the Cauvery of South India) at Omkareshwar.

There is also a belief in the inherent purity of the Ganga and its cleansing powers. But are there any scientific grounds for such a belief? I found that there was a fair amount of scientific research on this matter, suggesting that the water coming from the upper reaches of the river has natural self-purifying properties. Apart from higher dissolved oxygen and mineral content, ‘Ganga jal’ contains far more bacteriophages[1] (viruses that specifically attach and destroy bacteria) than rivers like the Narmada or the Yamuna. While pollution impacts the Ganga too[2], it does a fair job of cleaning itself. Be that as it may, as the river flows downstream, it is not as pure as in the upper reaches.

This makes me wonder if there is any truth to the claim that scientists confirm the existence of the Saraswati at Prayagraj. An online search threw up a 2019 article from The Hindu, confirming that scientists have uncovered a 45 kilometer long ancient river in Uttar Pradesh near Prayagraj,[3] as well as another news report from ETV Bharat.[4] I also found a NSRC/ISRO technical report from 2014[5] substantiating that the Saraswati was indeed a mighty river once, originating in the Himalayas and flowing between the Sutlej and the Yamuna. Its waters emptied into the Rann of Kutch. It was lost after a tectonic shift around 5000 years ago, and the water from large parts of the region that it drained now flow into the Sutlej, Yamuna, and Tons rivers.

ISRO’s remote sensing and GIS technologies have mapped the paleo channels of the river, showing us where the river once flowed, and attesting to the existence even in the present day of some underground aquifers along these channels. But, of the scientific experiment with the colours there is no mention anywhere – to my mind, most likely it is a modern myth based on the colour-coded maps of the study, which the undaunted layman has misinterpreted!

I muse upon how, at sunset, everyone converges on the many ghats at Prayagraj (and other places along the river) for the Ganga aarti. Huge ceremonial lamps are lit and the flames of faith shine bright against the darkening evening sky.

[1]RijulKochhar’s scholarly and deeply interesting paper explores the historical discovery of bactericidal action of the waters of the Ganga and Yamuna. See Kochhar, Rijul. The virus in the rivers: histories and antibiotic afterlives of the bacteriophage at the sangam in Allahabad. Notes and Records: the Royal Society Journal of the History of Science, 22 July 2020. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsnr.2020.0019. Accessed 24.03.2025.

[2] Municipal sewage and industrial effluents from 48 cities and 66 large towns aredischarged into the Ganga, according to a paper by environmental scientists Jhariya and Tiwari. See Jhariya, Dalchand & Tiwari, Anoop. (2020). Ganga River: A Paradox of Purity and Pollution in India due to Unethical Practice. IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environmental Science. 597. 012023. 10.1088/1755-1315/597/1/012023. Accessed 24.03.2025.

[3]https://www.thehindu.com/news/national/telangana/csir-ngri-study-unravels-45-km-long-buried-river-in-ganga-yamuna-region/article37966598.ece. Accessed 11.03.2023.

[4]https://www.etvbharat.com/english/bharat/evidence-of-saraswati-river-found-in-ganga-yamuna-doab-prayagraj/na20211217145756073. Accessed 11.03.2023.

[5]https://bhuvan-app1.nrsc.gov.in/saraswati/usertasks/saraswati/docs/Report_Saraswati_Integrated_Nov.2014.pdf. Accessed 11.03.2023.

Sheel is an author and editor. She can be reached at sheel.sheel@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sheel
From the sacred waters of the Ganga to the lost Saraswati, the Maha Kumbh Mela at Prayagraj earlier this year showcased how rivers shape our geography and our beliefs. Sheel shares the experience that drew millions of people.
